[Security and identity dependencies] Security and identity dependencies are often neglected. However, these are very critical factors for SAP migrations.

User authentication: Directory services (e.g., LDAP/Active Directory) handling user login for SAP ECC should be treated similarly to user login in the cloud environment. For many companies, SAP logons are integrated with Active Directory (by SSO or SAP Secure Login). Cloud migration does require (one of the following):

- Extend AD to the cloud (Configure a domain controller in AWS/Azure)

- Use services like Azure AD Domain Services

- Use SAML SSO with Azure AD or AWS SSO

Prepare in advance your user authentication strategy post-migration, and have the required cloud identity infrastructure.

[SAProuter and network security at SAP] For SAP support connections on-premise you apparently use SAProuter already; you need to do this in the cloud also. Consider either:

- SAProuter Cloud deployment

- Use SAP Support Cloud Connector

Note that the on-premise allowed partner or SAP IP ranges may also need to be allowed in the cloud firewall.

- Encryption/Key Management: If you have HSM (Hardware Security Module) or key stores in your on-premise environment for encryption, you will need to have the same in the cloud environment, too. For example,

- In case your SAP system uses the secure store for the RFCs

- If you have PKI integrated

You may have to transfer or integrate your keys as KMS (Key Management Service), HSM is provided by most cloud providers.

- Security policies and compliance: One is you might have your on-premise environment compliant with certain standards (ISO, HIPAA, etc), and you need to get another world cloud environment as per similar standards. As part of your dependency analysis, document your current security controls like:

- IP whitelisting at the network edge

- bastion servers for admin access

Each cloud provider, of course, has its own security strategy:

- AWS: security groups, NACLs

- Azure: NSGs and User-Defined Routes

[Summary]

Infrastructure dependencies tell you everything that your SAP servers need from your environment in order to work properly. To make sure that your desired cloud environment has all the necessary parts, document them. This deep-dive analysis of dependencies prevents potential problems during the migration and avoids nasty surprises after the migration.

Application-level dependencies

In addition to the infrastructure, SAP ECC systems usually connect to a wider application ecosystem. Such dependencies may exist at the application level - software and business processes that depend on (or are dependent on) the SAP system.

[Custom ABAP Code] There is a certain level of custom development (Z* programs, user exits, BADIs, extensions) on Most SAP ECC systems. Here are the reasons why these custom objects must be treated as dependencies.

- Quite often they were based on certain SAP data structures or functions, which change in S/4HANA. However, S/4HANA is a new product with a lot of simplified data models and changed transactions. As an example, in classical ERP, the tables MKPF/MSEG represent your inventory postings; however, in S/4HANA, these have been replaced by MATDOC tables. Any custom ABAP reports that are reading MKPF/MSEG directly will not work in S/4HANA without additional adjustment. Identify these changes with the SAP S/4HANA Simplification List (used by Readiness Check).

- Your custom code could be using deprecated modules or function modules. You scan your custom code for its dependency on S/4HANA. SAP itself gives tools (e.g. ABAP Test Cockpit and/or Custom Code Analyzer) to indicate where changes are needed.

- Make sure you account for data dependencies. If your custom programs rely upon something specific data (E.g custom Z-tables) you need to get these migrated.

- Batch jobs (SM36 jobs) utilizing Z programs need to be documented for post-migration testing.

- Custom code as an internal dependency linking to SAP standards. Correcting the custom code is a lot of work in a S/4HANA conversion project. Use checking tools in the early phase of planning.

[Third-Party Integrations]

- Upstream/downstream systems: Identify all external systems that communicate with SAP. For instance, staging is often done with a service such as sending financial data to an old data warehouse or getting orders from an API on an e-commerce platform. For each interface, document:

 - From where is the communication originating

 - Protocol (IDoc, RFC, REST, SOAP, file transfer)

 - Middleware involved

- Interfaces via IDoc or RFC: In case your plants or your partner systems are pushing IDocs (intermediate documents) to ECC, capture those partners. Though once migrated these partners might need some configuration to point to the new S/4HANA system. If you use RFC connections with stored credentials (CPIC), also consider updating those.

- APIs and Web services: ECC6 may have exposed SOAP web services through SAP PI/PO or directly through ICF services. It may have OData services on top of it if you already have a Gateway for Fiori. Identify all of the exposed endpoints. Also, identify external APIs that are called by ECC (e.g., to get exchange rates or credit ratings). Map out how you will connect each to your cloud environment.

 - If you are calling public APIs: Allow outbound internet access

 - In case you are calling services in your corporate network: Verify VPN connectivity

- Third-party add-ons: Do you use third-party add-ons or plugins in ECC (for example, Vertex to do tax calculations or archiving solutions)? Validate that your add-ons are compatible with S/4HANA. Some add-ons may have S/4HANA-enabled versions but will be no longer needed if S/4HANA itself has equivalent features built in. You can check for add-on compatibility issues with SAP's Readiness Check tool. If they do not work together, you have to find alternatives or eliminate them before the migration.

- Reporting and BI: Determine whether ECC flows data into Business Warehouse (BW) or other BI environments. It could be extractor jobs or SLT replication. Certain extractors are replaced during S/4HANA migration. Hence, your dependency analysis should also validate BW extractors and reporting. SAP Readiness Check will show exactly if and which BW extractors must be adjusted.

- Email, Print, Output: ECC may be configured to send mail via SMTP relay or print to a local print server. Think of these dependencies. Connect an SMTP service so your S/4HANA in the cloud can send email (either use a cloud email service or continue on-premise email via VPN) For printing, try one of the following approaches:

 - Print server on-premise connected by VPN

 - Set up a cloud printing solution

- Legacy data or file dependencies: E.g. SAP interfaces may work by dropping some files to a directory or loading files. An example would be where files from the upstream system are sent via SFTP to the ECC server and processed in batch jobs. Such directories and file transfers must be detected. However, for cloud environments, upon migration, you might need to rebuild your SFTP server or opt for a managed service such as AWS Transfer Family or Azure SFTP.

[Middleware and Orchestration] If your landscape contains middleware like SAP PI/Process Integration, SAP PO/Process Orchestration, or a third-party ESB (e.g. MuleSoft), find out how is it connecting to ECC.

- SAP PI/PO is on-premise and not used as is; plan for connecting to S/4HANA in the cloud. If you need to connect, note that the VPN must be on, and the associated ports (SOAP adapter port) must be open. You may also think about migrating the middleware itself to the cloud or substituting the middleware with a cloud integration service, like SAP Integration Suite or Azure Logic Apps. This decision, relative to how many interfaces will traverse the middleware, is key to this understanding.

- Batch Scheduler: If you use external schedulers (Redwood Cronacle, Autosys, etc.) into the scheduler for triggering SAP jobs, ensure they are able to connect to the new system or to plan a migration to the scheduler.

- API to Middleware: If ECC calls external web services (REST/SOAP) hosted in the middleware, in this case the source IP address and location will be changed after migration. Verify that the firewall rules on the middleware side have been bypassed in order for the cloud to connect.

[APIs and Extensions]

- Fiori Apps / SAP Gateway: If you are deploying SAP Fiori (embedded in SAP Gateway or ECC as a front-end server) on ECC, they will be part of S/4HANA (embedded Fiori is standard on S/4HANA). List the Fiori apps and OData services being used so that you can validate them after migration. Also, ensure that your SSO Fiori Identity Provider is functional in the new environment (such as Azure AD).

- SAP Cloud Connector: For any integration of ECC via SAP Cloud Platform services using SAP Cloud Connector, it might need to be reinstalled or reconfigured on the migration of ECC to the cloud. This connector is essential for integration with SAP Cloud Services (use cases connecting to SAP Cloud Services; SuccessFactors integration, Analytics Cloud data acquisition, etc.).

- Mobile or Web Apps: If you have custom mobile apps or web portals that query ECC via API, list them. However, after migration, we might have to point these URLs or IPs to the new S/4HANA box. This is still the configuration and DNS entries plan that these apps are able to access the new system.

[Business process dependencies] You need to look at process timing, not just technical connections.

- If ECC and another system exchange data on a regular basis, try to time the cutover so the data cycle is not interrupted. You’ll have to suspend one and let the other catch up. Awareness of these order dependencies (i.e. shipping schedules) will inform you of the timing of your migration (e.g. run certain migrations earlier, disable certain background jobs during migration, etc.).

- Master data synchronization: If ECC is the master for some data which is being copied in other systems, make sure this data is in sync after migration. For example, if product data are sent daily from ECC to a web shop, run an additional sync prior before shutting down ECC and another after S/4HANA startup so that the web shop does not miss any updates.

[Summary]

Open application-level dependency analysis makes sure that the S/4HANA system works seamlessly with the IT environment. This is the equivalent of mapping out all the touch points of the current ECC system. A common problem with cloud migration is overlooked integration. An example would be if an old accounting system was pulling flat files from ECC, the accounting system would stop functioning after ECC migration as it was not updated with the new target location. This will aid you in planning how to migrate and adapt any integrations, customizations, or usage that you all have regarding ECC, with a detailed record enabling you to do so easily.

Reading system architecture diagrams (if they exist) and talking to a variety of people involved in the system (integration teams, developers, Basis people, network engineers, business users, …) is a very useful exercise. This lets you accumulate “tribal knowledge” about what’s connected to what. For most projects, it really helps to schedule workshops with these teams to identify dependencies that might not get documented formally.

Tools for dependency analysis

Collecting this data manually is not easy. Luckily, several tools can help to analyze dependencies in SAP migrations. They could be the SAP-provided tools for assessing the S/4HANA-readiness of ECC systems, storage analysis tools, and cloud services that map infrastructure dependencies.

SAP Readiness Check

SAP Readiness Check is an essential tool for any customer wanting to get prepared for conversion to S/4HANA. You scan your current SAP ECC 6.x system and examine the building blocks of your S/4HANA conversion project.

[Main functions of SAP Readiness Check (RC) analysis]

- Add-on compatibility: Verify that SAP and third-party add-ons present in your ECC system are compatible with S/4HANA. It will identify incompatible components, and also call out dependencies that are a barrier to conversion.

- Active business functions: This confirms if business functions (like EA-FIN and Industry Specific functions) that were enabled in Switch Framework (SFW5) are available in S/4HANA or are automatically re-enabled. Some functions may have become obsolete as they are now native functions in S/4.

- Simplification Items: Checks system utilization against the Simplification Item Catalog, which details the changes between ECC and S/4HANA. For instance, you use Classic GL in ECC, which implies transforming to Universal Journal in S/4HANA. It clearly identifies what applies, what you must do to prepare, and what measures you need to adapt to.

- Custom Code Analysis: It detects custom ABAP objects that need adjustment in S/4HANA. Identifies deprecated transactions, direct table access, deletion APIs, etc., and thus contributes to the scope of remediation work and project timeline. (Disclaimer: This feature uses ABAP Test Cockpit results with some S/4HANA check variants. You need to have installed certain SAP Notes and run data collection and upload programs.)

- Data Volume and Sizing: Analyze existing DB size along with its growth trends to provide an estimate of SAP HANA sizing. Finds large tables and recommends archiving if needed. It helps understand the memory/disk space requirement of the target HANA system (for instance, the need for a 2TB HANA instance). It also provides suggestions for data volume management, such as the tables that consume the most space.

- SAP Custom Development Projects: Identifies and flags SAP Custom Development (formerly SAP Consulting solutions and custom objects) in the system.

- Integration Considerations: Specific integration points to check if require some changes - e.g., if you are using BW specific extractors or specific IDoc interfaces. For instance, bit of old logistics extractors have been deprecated in S/4HANA, this check will highlight those.

- Fiori Readiness and User Experience: Provides an overview of the SAP Fiori apps available for your scenario overall and you can see the UX (user-experience) aspects (it is not a dependency but useful planning information to see that there are apps that can function as replacement to your custom developed ones).

- Infrastructure Check: The infrastructure check verifies readiness, e.g., systems, whether or not ECC is already Unicode-enabled (a requirement for S/4HANA). However, the readiness of the network and OS is not beyond the basic checks, and it is the user's responsibility to make detailed checks.

- Suggestions: Shows a dashboard with items to be fixed before conversion sorted by type. The dashboard also separates what is mandatory from optional so that you can see the before and after state of the issues that must still be fixed before the S/4HANA migration.

Using the SAP Readiness Check would normally involve the following steps:

1) Pre-steps: Implementing SAP Notes on your ECC system

2) Data Collection: Runs data collectors to gather system information, custom code usage, etc.

3) Upload: Upload the collected data to the SAP Readiness Check web service

4) Report Generation: Build interactive reports

The SAP Note also states that executing the Readiness Check on a copy of the most recent production environment will yield the most accurate results. While this is a “high-level” tool, it is invaluable to start your migration. It shows you what you need to know in the many dependency areas - custom code, add-ons, data, etc.

The Readiness Check is focused mostly on application-level dependencies. It indicates what part of your ECC applications needs some change or will be affected by migration to S/4HANA. It doesn't give you direct info like external interfaces IPs but it does cover internal dependencies like installed components and readiness for custom code.

Sample: Such as, if while performing a Readiness check, it shows that a particular third-party add-on is not certified for S/4HANA, then this is an important dependency to resolve! You might have to develop a plan to get an update from the vendor or eliminate that add-on. Or if the Readiness Check shows 500 custom objects that need adjustments, then you know you need to block time for adjusting and testing these objects in your migration plan. This is because custom objects often need to be adjusted to accommodate new data model changes during conversion to S/4HANA. SAP Readiness Check is one of the important tools at our disposal when it comes to planning our migration to SAP S/4HANA, as it helps us to recognize what changes are necessary, what their impact will be, and how we can prepare and plan as a migration project.

SAP Solution Manager (and Maintenance Planner)

This is where SAP Solution Manager (SolMan) comes in, a super useful tool to plan and run conversion projects. Basis experts deal with SolMan on a regular basis for system monitoring and support and maintenance activities. In this blog post we will elaborate how SolMan could be used for dependency analysis for the planning purpose.

- Maintenance Planner: This is a cloud service from SAP (access from Launchpad), which replaced the traditional Solution Manager based "Maintenance Optimizer". When doing system conversions it has become a most valuable tool. You specify the components of your target S/4HANA system stack in Maintenance Planner, and it compares them with the landscape of your current system. It is responsible for checking whether ECC product versions, add-ons and installed components are convertible and creates the stack XML file to be used for the upgrade. Essentially, it is just a software component dependency checker. It confirms that all of the software packages that have been installed have a migration path towards S/4HANA and identifies those that are to be uninstalled prior to migration. If items are incompatible, then Maintenance Planner will not take you further in the process until the incompatible issues are addressed.

 - For instance, if your industry solution/add-on does not have its equivalent in S/4HANA, the maintenance planner will indicate this. You can either acquire a new version from the vendor or uninstall it. It is good to know this early.

 - It also makes sure that as per the conversion, you have all the files (once its done you will work with Software Download Center to get the S/4HANA installation files).

- SolMan Custom code Management: Solution Manager has features like Custom Code Lifecycle Management (CCLM) that can grab information about the custom objects and its usage. While many organizations use standalone tools such as ATC and Readiness Check for custom code analysis, if you already manage your custom code in SolMan, you can leverage data on which custom objects exist and how well they are being used. This facilitates analysis of the custom code that needs to be adapted (for example, prioritizing commonly used transactions).

- Focused Build / Process Documentation: If your organization is using SolMan for process and interface documentation as a by-product of either Solution Documentation or Focused Build, an inventory of business scenarios and interfaces can be available. This can serve as a guide as to what to focus on. If the documentation of the SolMan lists an interface from ECC to a banking system, it is a dependency that should be considered. While not all organizations are using SolMan for this goal, if they are, it represents a good source of information to support migration planning.

- Test Management: SolMan provides a Test Suite that will not directly map the dependencies but can be used to perform integration testing. Identification of dependencies - Once they are identified, write test cases for them (e.g., test each interface for migration, etc). Both these test cases can be managed in SolMan along with CBTA scripts, if available. It allows you to verify that each identified dependency functions correctly once the migration has been completed.

- System and integration monitoring: If you have technical monitoring with SAP Solution Manager (SolMan) configured, you can gain metrics and logs for the interface calls (IDoc monitoring, Web Services monitoring), if applicable. Another way to discover dependencies is through a view in the End-to-End (E2E) monitoring dashboard within SolMan, where you can see any external calls that are running through ECC. If we see regular IDoc errors on SolMan with a given partner in the error partner section, you know there are interfaces we need to work on.

[Summary]

In this context, the SAP Solution Manager serves as a helper for actual project realization. It validates whether the technical prerequisites and the necessary software dependencies for the conversion are satisfied (via Maintenance Planner) and serves the purpose of a conversion project management suite (documentation, test cases, etc.). It does not auto-discover dependencies for external systems but serves as a control center for the conversion. The Maintenance Planner is always recommended to be used as a best practice early on, to catch early conflicts of compatibility.

SAP also offers a Simplification Item Check (which is also integrated into the Readiness Check results) as an ABAP report, which you can execute in ECC (via program /SDF/RC_START_CHECK) to validate for specific configuration/dependency issues - and produces a list of simplification items that fall under the scope of the items to be resolved. This is always run from SolMan or directly from ECC, is used in conjunction with the above tools to perform an in-depth double-check, and is generally executed as part of the preparation steps.

NetApp BlueXP - for storage-level dependencies

NetApp tools can definitely help your organization in storage and data dependencies (particularly SAP filesystems and databases) in the event that your current environment uses NetApp storage, or if you plan to leverage NetApp solutions in the cloud like Azure NetApp Files or Amazon FSx for NetApp ONTAP.

NetApp BlueXP is a unified control plane that provides a single interface for management data in on-premises and multi-cloud environments (previously Cloud Manager and associated services). In the sections below, we will cover how BlueXP and other NetApp tools provide a path to perform dependency analysis and migration.

- Visualize storage usage: BlueXP connects to on-premise ONTAP systems and leverages low-level volume usage and capacity analysis to provide powerful insights with integrated file analysis and file classification. E.g., the detailed visibility of data volume and growth trends if SAP global file shares are on NetApp volumes. It is more centered around data analysis than dependency mapping, but we think it gives a holistic view of the data to be migrated. The BlueXP classification is used to detect duplicate data like ancient archives and to assess which data should not be part of the migration process, therefore making the migration more efficient.

- Storage Replication (SnapMirror): One of NetApp's best things is its migration with SnapMirror. If your SAP ECC databases or file systems are hosted on-premise NetApp storage, simple replication to a cloud target can be performed.

 - AWS environment: Block-level replication to the cloud is also possible when using Amazon FSx for NetApp ONTAP as the target. Using BlueXP, you can create a simple SnapMirror relationship between your on-premise NetApp cluster and FSx ONTAP.

 - Azure environment: Cross-region replication is possible to target Azure NetApp Files (ANF). BlueXP can orchestrate replication from your on-premise cluster to ANF.

 - Google Cloud environment: You can do the same with Google Cloud NetApp Volumes via Cloud Volumes ONTAP as the target.

- SnapMirror is capable of seamlessly maintaining your storage dependencies while accurately replicating file permissions and directory structures and data. This is especially helpful for migrating large databases or filesystems, and the time offline can be greatly reduced by repeatedly applying incremental updates after performing an initial bulk sync. The first copy is a full synchronization (full copy), and the subsequent is an automatic incremental mode that only transmits the difference in changes. To offer an idea, to migrate more than 10TB of data, once this amount of data is transferred, these reads can be performed and updated every night with an average downtime of 5 minutes - this means that you can keep SAP HANA global file shares (example) up to date. You can also set it up using the BlueXP GUI in 3 simple steps, with no need for advanced CLI skills.

1) Choose source/destination volumes

2) Sync interval (15 minutes to 24 hours)

3) Optimize encryption settings and transfer speed limits

- NFS/shared file dependency handling: If an ECC is using NFS mounts (most commonly a shared, /sapmnt, or ASCS shared disks in an HA cluster), Azure NetApp Files (ANF) or Amazon FSx for NetApp ONTAP in the cloud will provide similar feature. Azure NetApp Files for NFS and Amazon FSx for NetApp ONTAP offer high performance and high availability scalable to eliminate these shared file dependencies. (BlueXP helps you provision these services and migrate data to them.)

For instance, if you are operating ECC in an HA cluster environment that uses shared storage for ASCS Enqueue, Azure can adopt ANF in a "Standard HA" configuration. BlueXP helps you to set up this configuration and recapture the data.

If ECC writes interface files to a NAS share, you can ensure that applications that depend on these files continue to function when migrated by replicating the share to the cloud, which retains system integration without changing file paths.

- Blueprints and automation: BlueXP automates the deployment of SAP environments with NetApp storage in a cloud environment. BlueXP Workload Orchestrator and templates automate the deployment of NetApp cloud storage with the needed performance and configure replication schedules.

- Maintain the advantage of minimal downtime: If you syncing your storage ahead of time, you can greatly reduce downtime during final cutover. Availability of synchronized data in the cloud can efficiently address dependencies on SAP data. At cutover all that remains is for you to perform the last synchronization and cut over your SAP systems to the new environment.

- Support for non-NetApp storage: If you are not using NetApp products on the premises, you can still use BlueXP copy and sync service to copy data or sync from any other NAS or file systems to cloud storage. We also have incremental copies in place with BlueXP copy and sync, and it supports data migration from any general NFS servers to S3 buckets, EFS, etc.

NetApp BlueXP is a simplification of both storage management and migration. It is particularly important in large-scale SAP system migration. You are migrating SAP systems that often have tons of data in GB (can go to TB). Data transfer is very slow with traditional methods, but using storage-level tools like SnapMirror greatly accelerates the migration process.

BlueXP is a bridge between the on-premise environment (your server), and the cloud environment (server on the internet). It controls the files and folders required for the SAP system to function properly that they get organized similarly in the cloud environment.

Cloud provider proprietary tools (e.g. AWS, Azure, Google Cloud migration/dependency tools)

Every major cloud provider has their own tools to scan your existing environment for dependencies. They are not SAP specific, but rather general products that map infrastructure as well as application communication, and they map the communication between the systems. They are very helpful in a “lift and shift” and are complementary to the SAP-specific tools shown above.

AWS Migration Hub and Application Discovery

AWS offers the Migration Hub, a centralized management service for migrations, and the following discovery tools:

- AWS Application Discovery Service (ADS): It has two modes: Agentless for VMware environments and Agent-based for all servers. In the Agentless mode, this connects to the vCenter to pull information about the VMs (config, performance) and uses network information to report on communication between the VMs. In Agent-based mode, a lightweight agent is deployed on each server, which monitors and logs things like running processes, open ports, inbound/outbound connections, etc. Push this data to Migration Hub, where you can visualize which applications were grouped.

Map dependencies with discovery data. As an example, if you see that an IP of an SAP ECC server communicates frequently with another known external system (such as a web portal or tax calculation server) on a known port, you would find that as a dependency to remediate. Based on the observed communication, Migration Hub can then group servers into applications.

- AWS Migration Hub Strategy Recommendations: This new feature can analyze application dependencies and recommend migration strategies for every re-host, re-platforming, etc. It is not SAP-specific but can classify SAP servers and their links.

- AWS Service Catalog / Well Architected Tool: Not a dependency graph tool exactly, but worth mentioning is AWS’s blueprints and best practices for SAP on AWS; once you know what dependencies you have you can use this to shape your AWS environment (VPC, subnets, etc.) so all the bits fit.

All necessary servers are migrated at the same time during migration planning by AWS migration utilities. This stops you from moving an application server and then forgetting that it talks to an on-prem file server on a custom port. AWS’s auto-mapping tool will find dependencies that humans often don’t realize exist, particularly at scale. One such example of cloud-agnostic focus is dependency mapping tools, which usually highlight servers that should be migrated together to avoid application disruption.

AWS offers the AWS Migration Acceleration Program (MAP) and works with specialized tools (for example, SNP CrystalBridge for SAP and Panaya for SAP analytics) that can help provide more detailed insights into code and data migration planning. But if we consider only AWS's own services, Migration Hub and ADS (Application Discovery Service) is a great choice.

AWS Migration Hub provides a single place to discover existing servers, plan migrations, and track the status of each application migration. This provides greater transparency into your application portfolio and makes planning and tracking less cumbersome. Migration Hub is integrated with other tools, including the AWS Application Migration Service and the AWS Database Migration Service.

Azure Migrate and Dependency Visualization

Microsoft has Azure Migrate as a migration services hub focused on the cloud, like AWS Migration Hub, and as a part of Azure Migrate discovery and assessment, it has dependency analysis as an option.

- Agentless Dependency Analysis: Uses a deployed VM virtual appliance (for VMware/Hyper-V) on your environment. The appliance gathers data by passively monitoring network traffic (gaining the TCP connection data) only without having to deploy agents to your servers. Group by process that initiated the connection, destination, port, etc. You can even visualize a dependency map for each server or group after hours or days of it keeping an eye on you. You can see here, for instance, SAP's ECC server has outgoing connections on port 443 to external IPs (connections to cloud services possible) and incoming connections on port 50000 (RFC possible from other apps). You can export the results in CSV as well.

- Discover using Agent-based Dependency (Service Map): By installing Microsoft Monitoring Agent and Dependency Agent in each server, this provides you with more fine-grained information (Process Name, Service). This works on any server, be it physical or other cloud environments. The dependency agent streams this telemetry to the Azure Monitor Service Map. You can see the topology in Azure Migrate.

- These tools specifically assist with making sure that “nothing is left behind” in the migration as you have to get everything right if you want to avoid outages as Microsoft says they’ll find all the connections.

- Use case: E.g., SAP’s ECC has an external interface to a bank’s system via SFTP. So, the dependency tool would show an outgoing connection from the ECC server to an external IP on port 22. It does give you a hint as to what it is (likely a scheduled SFTP transfer), which you may have overlooked. You can now check that the S/4HANA server in Azure reaches the bank IP (over the Internet or ExpressRoute) and that keys and credentials are migrated.

- Azure Migrate Cloud Assessment: In addition to mapping, Azure Migrate also uses discovered configurations to provide features such as recommending VM sizes in Azure and estimating costs. On the dependencies side, you can specify what servers are talking to each other and create “move groups.” You can, for example, group a SAP app, its DB server, and any legacy servers they may hook to and migrate it all in a single step.

- There is also an "SAP on Azure guide" for Azure, which frequently refers to an initial usage of Azure Migrate for discovery and then calls for specific SAP deploy services.

Google Cloud Migrate for Compute Engine (and Migration Center)

Migrate for Compute Engine (formerly Velostrata) is Google Cloud's main migration tool and is designed to enable VMs to move to Google Cloud with little to no downtime. Google Cloud also provides discovery and assessment tools, including:

- (Newly Introduced) Google Cloud Migration Center: Assessment also includes network dependency analysis and a report feature. For the Migration Center, a collector needs to be installed, or data can be imported from other tools. You will see all connections going to, and out from all assets (servers) and the number of connections, etc. It is similar to dependency mapping in Azure/AWS.

- Google documentation suggests building a “dependency matrix” of your workloads before migration, which is precisely the output of these tools - what other systems each server is communicating with.

- While Migrate for Compute Engine itself does test cutovers and has a runbook style of approach, they do dependency mapping first via Migration Center or third-party tools.

- Google Cloud provides SAP on Google Cloud offerings with its best practices in the SAP context. Similar to how Google tends to team up with Atos or Accenture, for example, when migrating SAP, these organizations may promote their own frameworks to review dependencies or depend on the SAP Readiness Check and some generic tools.

- If your organization uses Google Cloud, there is also StratoZone (a discovery tool acquired by Google) that assists in both discovery and cost estimation, including capturing downstream application dependencies.

To summarize, Google Cloud Migration Center is a dependency mapping tool (similar to Azure Migrate/AWS ADS) and should be utilized for accounting for each and every component of your SAP landscape (as well as connected systems).

Open Source and Other Tools

In addition to provider-specific tools (Azure Migrate, AWS ADS, Google Cloud Migration Center), there exist vendor-agnostic open-source solutions as well.

- Application Dependency Mapping (ADM) tools: Tools like Device42 with discovery capabilities, CMDB tools, and Faddom can help map the dependencies of applications running across hybrid environments.

- Assess infrastructure as code: If your environment is defined with Terraform, VMware scripts, etc, you can also look at those and find dependencies.

- Custom Script: One can also use netstat or ss commands in Linux or Resource Monitor in Windows to obtain raw data regarding existing connections. Many admins create scripted processes to pull all of this info from all servers for cross references. It’s not as pretty as a tool dedicated to the task (and this is for a number of reasons), but it’s a quick way to figure out, say, if your SAP server is making a connection to some host on some weird port every night at 2 am (which would imply some dependency, like a batch job connecting to a vendor system).

Takeaway: Use the appropriate tool for your target environment. If migrating to Azure, using Azure Migrate dependency analysis, AWS uses AWS ADS; Google Cloud uses Migration Center. It’s because each cloud’s tool has been optimized for its own migration and integrates with the next steps on that platform. The output of their cloud tools is also well known if you have an Azure/AWS/Google Cloud expert to consult. These tools effectively do similar things (network and process retrieval); whichever you choose to use should yield a decent mapping of the entities your SAP system interacts with.

A summary of the roles and features of the tools to assist with SAP system migration

- SAP Readiness Check: Checks for dependencies the SAP software and custom code have, and suggests corrective measures to migrate to S/4HANA from ECC, helping to identify problems in advance before migration.

- SAP Solution Manager (also Maintenance Planner): Make sure compatibility between software components and efficiently coordinate the entire migration effort through project management and test management capabilities.

- NetApp BlueXP: Replication of SAP data (files and volumes) to the cloud and availability providing the same storage environment. Enables cloud migration while preserving data integrity.

- AWS / Azure / Google Cloud Migration Tools: Visualize communication dependencies between infrastructure and applications providing arrangement of layouts alongside network connections ensuring that servers are set up accurately in cloud environments.

- Specialized tools (SNP CrystalBridge, Panaya…etc.): Provide a comprehensive analysis of your SAP migration, including process analysis and test scope optimization. Although potentially beyond the purview of Basis administrators in the early planning phases, utilizing a specialized vendor should be considered if the budget is available.

By combining these tools, you get a full picture of your migration project. For instance, SAP Readiness Check to look into your SAP systems, Azure Migrate to map infrastructure dependencies, and NetApp SnapMirror (and BlueXP) to migrate your data, which gives you a clear picture of what you need to do to migrate.

AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud dependency management comparison

When it comes to hosting SAP S/4HANA, each cloud platform offers its set of distinctive features influencing dependency management. In this blog, we compare AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud and discuss how each of the platforms stacks up when it comes down to important dependency areas like networking, security, storage, and database migration so you have an idea of the coordination and planning you can expect to do on each of the platforms.

The purpose of such comparison is not to prefer one cloud provider over another, but rather to analyze what are the differences between them, i.e. AWS vs Azure will lead to different network design choices to establish connectivity to SAP systems.

Assessing Network Differences and Needs

Each of the three major cloud providers has the idea of a private virtual network that virtual resources can attach to, but they have subtle differences in the name or working.

- AWS: At AWS, use a region-specific Virtual Private Cloud (VPC). You can create subnets in your VPC and associate them with Availability Zones. Internal communication between all instances in a VPC is enabled by default. AWS has two types of subnets: public (as in, it can access Internet Gateway) and private. For SAP, the typical pattern would be to put the application and DB servers (no direct Internet access) into a private subnet and bastion hosts to provide admin access in a public subnet.

- Azure: Use region-specific VNet for Azure. In VNet, define subnets (Azure actually has Availability Zones, although there is no need to create separate subnets per AZ unless you need it since distribution is not carried out according to zones by name). By default, any resources in a VNet can talk to each other. Unlike AWS, Azure doesn't inherently have the concept of public/private subnets, and any subnet can route to an Internet Gateway depending only on whether you give your VM a public IP. For SAP, consider one VNet per region or environment and then use network security groups to control the traffic.

- Google Cloud: Intended for a global model (takes multiple regions), but it also uses VPC. For new VPC (Auto Mode VPC), Google Cloud, by default, creates one subnet in every region. Companies that are looking to have full control over subnets mostly tend to use Custom Mode. A subnet is present in each region within a VPC, and instances in the same VPC can communicate across regions over our backbone. SAP can utilize multiple regions (Say DR in another region), and Google Cloud guarantees global producers and consumers communication via global VPC, adding no cost for VPC peering across regions compared to AWS/Azure.

Connectivity to on-premises and external systems

There are multiple options for all clouds:

- AWS: Hybrid scenario connectivity is generally via VPN or AWS Direct Connect. If you need to keep some systems on-premises, such as older systems that cannot be migrated, you will need a VPN or Direct Connect link so that S/4HANA on AWS can communicate with it with low latency. If your integration flows a large amount of data (like real-time data replication), AWS Direct Connect provides a high-bandwidth private connection.

- Azure: Offers VPN and ExpressRoute for private connectivity. Enterprises commonly use ExpressRoute lines as a stable, high-speed line between Azure and on-premise data centers. If the SAP ECC system is connected with lots of on-premise systems, investing in an express Route will provide a more stable performance.

- Google Cloud: They have VPN and Cloud Interconnect which works like Azure’s ExpressRoute. Moreover, Google Cloud, via third-party provider, has its own option, Partner Interconnect. When migrating SAP systems to Google Cloud, it is important to ensure secure, high-performance connectivity with on-premises systems that cannot be migrated immediately, such as LDAP servers.

Network Services and DNS

- AWS: AWS allows you to use Route 53 as your DNS, and you can create a private DNS zone that reflects your on-premises DNS name so that your SAP servers on AWS can resolve on-premise hostnames (for example, hr-system.corp.local” you point to your on-premise IPs through the Route 53 resolver endpoint.

- Azure: Azure uses Azure DNS (and Azure Private DNS zones) similarly. Azure can associate a VNet with a Private DNS Zone to help resolve the names of resources. If SAP is expecting certain hostnames (i.e. integration with certain hosts) you may wish to regenerate them in Azure Private DNS to now point to new IPs if required.

- Google Cloud: Google Cloud's DNS is Cloud DNS, which also allows private DNS zones. Google Cloud also supports global load balancing but this is for web apps only and not needed for SAP unless you expose some service to the web.

Peering and Service Endpoints

If you have an SAP system that uses cloud-native services (for example, storing a document in S3 or Azure Blob, or call AI services), think about your network configuration.

- AWS: Connect VPC endpoints as services can keep traffic within the AWS network (e.g. S3) or if you separate SAP into multiple VPCs (e.g. SAP in separate VPC and other apps) connect using VPC peering or Transit Gateway.

- Azure: VNet peering allows VNet to be connected (low latency, but pay attention to the cost of data transfer between regions). If something in a VNet needs to access SAP in another VNet, then plan those peerings and the NSG rules accordingly. On Azure there is also a Private Link to create the same access for PaaS services, like Azure SQL and Azure Storage.

- Google Cloud: Supports VPC peering and Shared VPC (Another concept where multiple projects use the same VPC - ideal for large organizations that want to centralize their networks). For dependencies, if you are using Shared VPC, ensure that there is communication enabled between your projects.

High-Availability Network Connectivity

SAP needs highly available network connections.

- AWS: Ensure that your VPC spans multiple availability zones (AZs) and deploy your SAP application servers in at least 2 AZs. Design it to be able to recover from AWS failures (e.g. if using HANA and implementing HANA System Replication for high availability - place primary on one AZ and secondary on another AZ).

- Azure: Similarly, use Availability Zones (if available in the region for the desired VM size). Place redundant components in separate zones (or at least separate failure domains if zones are not used).

- Google Cloud: While Google Cloud’s global networking approach inherently provides connectivity between regions, it uses multiple zones for intra-region high availability.

Load balancer

Your SAP setup would not have a Web Dispatcher or SAProuter. Instead, you would have a Cloud Load Balancer, Azure Traffic Manager, etc. However, these components serve different functions and purposes.

Consider the following traffic handling done by a SAP system:

- SAPGUI traffic is processed by SAP’s own message server and enqueue service

- SAP Web Dispatcher or the Fiori front-end server handles the web traffic

In order to improve redundancy, multiple web dispatchers are often placed behind cloud load balancers by many companies. This is a critical design decision and something you will want to document in your dependency analysis. For instance, if a user accesses SAP ECC by calling a certain URL, the same access can be achieved by configuring the DNS name of the load balancer pointing to the web dispatcher VM in the cloud environment. For SAP services deployed across multiple regions, DNS-based load balancing solutions like Azure Traffic Manager assist with achieving high availability. You can continuously monitor the health of endpoints and automatically failover traffic in the event of a failure. These components should be selected depending on the nature of traffic (such as HTTP or SAP GUI) and the type of network services needed (like SSL termination).

Differences in security (network)

Security is closely related to network configuration. Think about network security group rules to limit access and secure communication.

What distinguishes security from identity management?

Every cloud provider has a slightly different model for securing all of the resources you can provision through their platform, which will influence how you implement the security dependencies you've identified (firewall rules, user access, etc).

- AWS - AWS VPC’s Security Groups and NACLs: AWS VPC uses Security Groups (SGs) as stateful firewalls bound to instances. They are designed to be highly application-centric. For example, create an SG named "SAP-App-SG" and permit port access for 3200/TCP from everywhere in your VPC (or a specific SG, like a jump box SG). AWS SGs simplify allowing traffic from one group of servers to another; just need to reference the SG, so you don't have to worry about IPs. AWS also has network ACLs (stateless filters) for broader subnets, but these are typically left at their default settings if SGs are sufficient.

For example, in the case of SAP migrations, you can create security groups for SAP application servers, SAP HANA DB, and SAProuter (if any) and allow the relevant traffic. For instance, open SAP App SG to DB SG on port 30015 (HANA SQL port) and 30013 (HANA internal services communication) and open end-user subnetworks (or load balancer SG) to App SG on port 443 (Fiori HTTPS) or 3200 (SAP GUI). The model is low maintenance and functions like any filtering applied to internal firewall rules.

- AWS Identity: AWS IAM is a service that is used to grant permissions to AWS resources. IAM allows you to have granular control over who can access what resources in AWS. IAM allows you to create users, groups, and roles, granting the appropriate permissions and thereby allowing security management under the principle of least privilege. SAP applications generally have their own user management system, or they depend on Active Directory (AD) to enforce Access Control at the Application level. The AWS IAM is hardly even directly involved in user authentication for these SAP applications but does get involved in the following scenarios:

 - AWS services integration with S/4HANA: S/4HANA uses IAM roles to access AWS services, like Amazon S3. As an example, when an SAP process writes to an S3 bucket, it uses the EC2 instance profile to get temporary credentials for the IAM role, and thus access the bucket in a secure way.

 - AWS machine learning services: If S/4HANA calls AWS machine learning services, an IAM role is needed to read the training data from S3

- For SAP systems, a good option for single sign-on is AWS IAM Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO).

 - SAML-based: AWS IAM Identity Center supports the SAML 2.0 protocol integration with SAP Fiori Launchpad, enabling single sign-on for both AWS and SAP applications after one-time authentication.

 - Integration with existing identity providers: AWS IAM Identity Center can work with your existing identity providers, such as Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD), Okta, Ping, or on-premises Active Directory, to centrally manage access to SAP systems via these identity providers.

- Azure - Network Security Groups and Azure AD: Azure has its own equivalent for AWS SGs and it is known as Azure NSGs (Network Security Groups). NSGs are stateful firewalls that can be applied at either the subnet level or NIC level. You will usually add NSG rules to permit traffic. For Instance, an NSG attached to an SAP application subnet may permit traffic to certain ports inbound from Azure Firewall (if deployed) or from on-premise Subnets through ExpressRoute. In Azure, NSG rules lack the grouping by tags from AWS SGs, and because of that, you often just allow IP ranges or subnets explicitly in Azure.

- Application Security Groups (ASG) in Azure, which allows you to tag your NICs and refer to those tags in your NSG rules similar to SG's in AWS, which can make your rules less complex (for example, tag all your SAP App VMs with the ASG "SAP-App", your DB's with the "SAP-DB", then in your NSG rules allow communication from SAP-App to SAP-DB on the DB port).

- Centralized control: Many companies use Azure Firewall or Network Virtual Appliance through Firewall rules if dependencies like external interfaces are routed, consider firewalling too (e.g. SFTP open to specific partners).

- Azure AD: The identity platform in Azure plays an important role. It can be used for SAML SSO to SAP if the user is in Azure AD. Also, if ECC has been using on-prem AD, then while migrating them to cloud, they can deploy AD domain controllers in Azure VMs or maintain a domain for SAP utilizing Azure AD Domain Services. One approach is using replica domain controller VMs in Azure for domain join of SAP application servers.

- In addition to being an OAuth token issuer for custom services, for SAP, the primary integration is via SSO. In case you are on SAP Cloud Identity (SCI/IAG) you can integrate with Azure AD etc.

- Google Cloud Firewall Rules and Google Identities: At the VPC level firewall rules enforce the security on instances (those can be tagged or permissions provided to service accounts). Similar to AWS SG, Google Cloud's firewall rules are stateful, and they can be applied using tags. So for example, you could tag your SAP VMs with "sap-app" and write a rule that lets "sap-db" talk to "sap-app" on a specific port.

- Google Cloud extensively uses service accounts to identify VMs and firewall rules can also be defined based on service accounts (e.g., "any VM with service account X"), which you can use to separate roles between application and database VMs.

- Google Cloud identity management for users can be integrated with corporate identities through Google Cloud Directory Sync or SAML. Generally, you can still use SAML with SAP authentication if you have already installed AD or used Azure AD.

- If you are using Google Workspace User and want to integrate with SAP Logon, then Google Cloud identities are more relevant.

- Google Cloud IAM, like AWS IAM, controls access to Google Cloud, and has no direct impact on SAP, but if you use GCS buckets/BigQuery from SAP, you might need to manage service account keys or use workload identities.

- Security in data transit: All clouds support VPN encryption (IPsec), and once in the cloud, traffic in your VPC/VNet is typically not encrypted by default (as it is on a private network). If internal in-transit encryption is required, it may be necessary to use SSL/TLS for SAP-DB communication (HANA supports encryption) or set up an IPsec tunnel between VMs (the latter is rarely done internally). Cloud network isolation is usually sufficient (sniffing a cloud virtual network is harder than sniffing data center wiring).

- Security Compliance: All cloud providers have a "shared responsibility model". When using SAP systems, the customer is responsible for OS and application security (OS patching, SAP hardening). In contrast, maintaining the security of the physical and underlying infrastructure falls on the cloud provider. The differences are as follows:

- Patch frequency: Azure may automatically patch the Windows OS if opted in; however, with SAP systems, you usually manage patching it yourself.

- Antivirus: If you had antivirus agents on-premise, you would also have to deploy antivirus agents on your cloud VMs.

- Logs and monitoring: Cloud providers offer different log services (for instance, AWS VPC Flow Logs, Azure NSG flow logs). These can be used to track whether any unwanted dependencies are attempting to access your systems. For instance, if something keeps trying to connect after migration and failing to, you may be able to discover a dependency you missed.

In summary:

Security and identity management practices need to be translated accordingly when transitioning from on-premise to cloud. For example, a firewall rule, such as "System A can access System B on port 50000", when on-premise is transformed into a manner that is compatible with every provider within the cloud environment. In AWS, it is a security group rule; in Azure, it is a network security group (NSG) rule; and in Google Cloud, it is a firewall rule. Likewise, in the case of user authentication - whether you used Active Directory on-premise, you had the same challenge in the cloud once you decided to migrate: extend your existing AD to the cloud environment or build custom solutions through the identity service provided by the cloud provider as well to achieve the same single sign-on… Such conversion work needs to be performed for the end goal of ensuring that the security and access management is consistent from the viewpoint of users and integrated systems irrespective of the change in underlying technology and implementation method.

Things to consider when migrating storages (Amazon FSx for NetApp ONTAP vs Azure NetApp Files vs others)

Storage is a key element for SAP, not only for databases, but also for file systems and backups. We discussed storage in the overview of BlueXP, but now we can compare each service.

AWS Storage for SAP

- Block Storage: AWS uses EBS (Elastic Block Store) as the disk for VMs. SAP application server and HANA DB server use EBS volumes. There are different performance tiers (gp3, high IOPS io1/io2). HANA requires constant throughput, which can be achieved by striping multiple EBSs or using io2. AWS provides guidance on EBS sizing for HANA.

- Shared file system: Amazon EFS (Elastic File System) can be used as NFS shared storage (e.g. /sapmnt and interface file uses if required). But EFS is general purpose, and its performance is based on size and burst credits. The best option to use if you need higher performance (like /data, /log, SAP global transport directory, and /sapmnt for many applications servers) is Amazon FSx for NetApp ONTAP. FSx ONTAP offers fully POSIX-compliant NFS (or SMB/iSCSI) with guaranteed IOPS and advanced features. Significantly, FSx ONTAP is SAP certified for HANA workloads and meets performance and reliability standards. You can even make instant snapshots/clones of HANA data files, which is great for refresh and backup strategies.

- FSx ONTAP brings you closer to an on-premise NetApp environment. As discussed in the previous section, data can be migrated by copying on-premise volumes to FSx ONTAP using SnapMirror. Note: This is applicable if you keep your HANA data and logs on FSx volumes (some architectures use NetApp storage to persist HANA data/logs for the full backups).

- You can also place HANA on EBS (as is common in many AWS deployments) in a non-NetApp environment, which means migration would be via data export and HANA replication rather than SnapMirror.

- Object storage for backups: Amazon S3 is commonly utilized for HANA backups (using backint agent or custom scripts) and data archiving. If dependency analysis shows that you have shared files with large amounts of archived content, moving them to S3 will be much cheaper in the long term than expensive block storage.

- Replacing Tape Backup: If you were using a tape system, AWS offers Storage Gateway Service and third-party solutions can also help with tape integration.

Azure Storage for SAP

Block Storage

Azure uses Managed Disks for virtual machine storage. Common ones include the followings:

 - Premium SSD: Up to 20,000 IOPS per disk

 - Ultra SSD: Very high IOPS/low latency, but not available in all regions or VM sizes

 - Standard SSD/HDD: Rarely used for databases

For Azure, for SAP HANA: For SAP HANA, Azure typically recommends Ultra Disk for transaction logs and Premium SSD for data depending on requirements. Alternatively, a stripe set of Premium SSDs can be used to meet throughput.

Shared File Storage

Azure NetApp Files(ANF): It is Azure's prime service for SAP. ANF is an Azure first-party service offering high-performance NFS (and SMB) volumes developed with NetApp. Since ANF is super-fast, it is being highly utilized for SAP in Azure, simplifying the SAP cluster setup (e.g. SAP global /sapmnt and HANA data can be hosted on ANF). ANF volumes can be resized no downtime and have different performance tiers. It also supports NetApp snapshots and replication. These are two important scenarios: one is HANA DBs backup using NetApp SnapCenter to ANF, and another is ANF cross-region replication for DR.

- For Migration from NetApp: SnapMirror to ANF is supported. However, current Azure policy recommends using a one-time data copy or HANA backup/restore.

- Azure Files: General file-share, not recommended for high IOPS, can be used for small shares (like interface file drops), but ANF is probably better for core SAP shared disks.

Backup and Archive

Azure Backup: There is an Azure Backup service, but this is done for SAP HANA using the SAP HANA plugin for Azure Backup or via a third party. Backups (e.g., backint from HANA to Azure Blob) are often used with Azure Blob Storage. If you are relying on some existing backup software that you were using on-premise (e.g., NetApp SnapCenter, CommVault), look for integration with Azure storage or consider moving to a cloud-native backup approach.

Large Instance Options

SAP HANA Large Instances: Azure has dedicated bare metal servers (SAP HANA Large Instances) for large-scale HANA workloads. All of these servers are equipped with dedicated storage (mainly NetApp AFF series). Selecting this environment requires dependency analysis because the data is in a special environment. However, a typical S/4HANA migration usually assumes the use of a virtual machine (VM).

Google Cloud Storage for SAP

Block Storage

Google Cloud offers three types of storage for VMs called "Persistent Disks (PD)".

- Standard (standard)

- SSD (high speed)

- Extreme (super high speed)

In particular, extreme PD delivers very high performance and is ideal for large systems at the HANA scale. The SAP HANA architecture best practices on Google Cloud recommends multiple PD volumes with LVM striping to achieve the required throughput. Keep in mind that Google Cloud's PDs are only available in a single zone by default. However, if a VM fails over to another zone, you need a replication setup (or regional PD, which will sync in multiple zones).

Shared Storage

For NFS shares, there is "Filestore" on Google Cloud. However, Filestore has generally been less scalable than ANF (Azure NetApp Files) or EFS (Amazon Elastic File System) under high load.

With regard to the second item, a first-party service known as Google Cloud NetApp Volumes (which is very similar to ANF, a managed service directly co-sold by Google and its partner, NetApp) recently became available on Google Cloud. Google Cloud NetApp Volumes - High-performance NFS and is highly suitable for migrations from NetApp environments (SnapMirror transfer via Cloud Volumes ONTAP is also supported).

backup

Many SAP on Google Cloud customers utilize Google Cloud Storage (GCS) for backups. Google Cloud Storage (GCS): SAP provides backint interface (through partner solutions or scripts). You can use Google Cloud's Transfer Appliance if you really need to ship lots of data, but in most cases, uploading over the network works.

Features of Google Cloud

One of the most popular features of Google Cloud storage is a snapshot feature for Persistent Disk. Consequently, PD snapshots can be performed quickly and incrementally. Some users use this as a backup, yet databases such as HANA require proper timing of snapshots to ensure consistency.

Storage Migration Strategy

Migration of database from one cloud vendor to another is difficult, but if well planned, it can be achieved successfully. We will go through some different database migration scenarios below and the right approach to use.

Conversion of the database (i.e. migration from/simply another database)

In the case of migration from one type of database to another (like Oracle to HANA), proprietary SAP tools should be used. For example, if you want to change the database type (from an Oracle database to a HANA database), you cannot just do a simple storage-level copy. Instead, use below SAP tool:

- SAP export/import tools: Get data out and pull it back into the new environment

- Software Update Manager (SUM) with DMO: Both migration and upgrade happen in a single go

If you are using these tools, you can complement the Storage Tool with:

- Moving exported data

- Reduce downtime by DB replication of a source DB in the course of building a target system

Migrating an existing HANA database

When migrating an existing HANA database to the Cloud, use HANA System Replication. Do you need to upgrade an existing HANA database from Suite on HANA to S/4HANA? Follow these steps:

1) Create a secondary HANA instance in the cloud environment.

2) Configure HANA System Replication (HSR) and initiate sync.

3) Once the data is synced sufficiently, switch the production environment.

It requires a good network connection; however, it's much better than trying with backup/restore methods.

One-step migration (ECC anyDB to S/4HANA)

If you are coming directly from ECC to S/4HANA, the process can be streamlined using SUM DMO. The one-step conversion of ECC (anyDB) to S/4HANA (HANA DB) on the cloud is done in the following ways:

1) Use SAP Software Update Manager with Database Migration Option (SUM DMO)

2) Use the option "Target Database Over Network"

3) The old database is exported by SUM and loaded on HANA over the network

This approach requires a robust network connection, but is more efficient because it eliminates additional export/import steps.

File and document migration

Dedicated synchronization tools or cloud appliances can be efficient options for file system data migration. As an example, to migrate data from an SAP content server or file system, the following tools can be used:

- rsync (for Linux/Unix)

- Robocopy (for Windows)

- Azure Data Box (for high-volume data)

If your bandwidth is limited you may also want to use the following transfer appliances offered by various cloud providers:

- AWS Snowball

- Azure Data Box

- Google Cloud Transfer Appliance

Cloud storage comparison

Each cloud provider offers storage services that leverage NetApp ONTAP.

AWS

- Amazon FSx for NetApp ONTAP: Provides functionality equivalent to an on-premise NetApp environment

- SnapMirror for direct volume replication

- multi-protocol support (NFS/SMB)

Azure

- Azure NetApp Files (ANF): NetApp services fully integrated into Azure Ecosystem

- Creates ANF volumes directly from the Azure Portal

- Data Migration with NetApp tools or NFS mount copy

Google Cloud

- Google Cloud NetApp Volumes: NetApp services fully integrated into Google Cloud environment

- Use as SnapMirror target via Cloud Volumes ONTAP

Selection Considerations

The choice of storage is determined by performance needs and operational requirements.

Storage selection considerations

- Make it simple: Native cloud storage (EBS/Managed Disks etc.)

- Focusing on performance, and snapshot/clone capabilities: NetApp based solution

- Backup/DR planning: Repurpose SnapMirror used for the migration during DR replication or utilize cloud snapshots or Backint

Summary

Your method will depend on the type of database you want to migrate, the environment you are migrating to, your downtime requirement, etc. Choosing the right migration tool/technique combination and investing enough time in planning and preparing for the migration can make migrating your database a smooth and secure process.

Migrating Database (SAP HANA and non-HANA DB)

Database migration is one of the key elements when migrating to S/4HANA. The on-premise version of S/4HANA comes on top of a common database platform, the SAP HANA DB, so if ECC 6.0 is currently on a database other than HANA (e.g., Oracle, DB2, SQL Server, MaxDB, etc.), then a database platform migration to HANA is required. This has significant implications for your migration plan.

Scenario

- ECC on non-HANA -> S/4HANA on HANA: Typical brownfield conversion scenario. It needs migration from the current database into SAP HANA. Using SAP's SUM (Software Update Manager) with DMO, this can be achieved in a single step. Alternatively, a two-step approach would be to first migrate the ECC database to HANA (Suite on HANA configuration, still an ECC application) and then upgrade the S/4HANA application. One-step migration is preferred whenever it is feasible in order to save time.

- ECC on HANA -> S/4HANA on HANA: For systems that already run HANA as a database, the core of the migration is an application upgrade. Here are the options for migrating HANA to the Cloud.

 - Migrate to the cloud firstly with HANA System Replication (HSR) with as little downtime as possible, and then upgrade to S/4HANA

- Backup/restore approach (Prolonged downtime occurs)

- New implementation (Greenfield): If you are creating a new S/4HANA environment, you start it on the HANA database from scratch. You migrate data using ETL tools, SAP Data Services, etc. This is a different approach than migrating from an existing system.

Implications for migration strategy

Software Update Manager (SUM) with DMO: This is the main brownfield migration tool. This tool performs the following tasks:

- Pull data from source databases

- Convert data format if necessary

- Upgrade to S/4HANA and submit data to HANA database at the same time

Using DMO (Database Migration Option) with System Move option, you can perform data extraction in an on-premise environment and the data can be submitted directly to the target system in a cloud environment.

- Pros: Comprehensive process with less manual work

- Cons: High processing load, needs stable network and pre-testing

If you have low network speed, you can export the data files and physically carry them with you, but normally another method is recommended.

It is recommended to test DMO in a sandbox to assess the migration effort and identify problems early on.

SAP migration with the classical heterogeneous system copy

A classic heterogeneous system copy is a migration method based on the export and import of the database through SWPM (Software Provisioning Manager). Export ECC data to a file and load the data to the installed S/4HANA. Since the data structures of ECC and S/4HANA are different, simple data migration is not possible. Due to the structure of the data, even loading the ECC data as is will not function properly after S/4HANA is installed. Thus, the classical way goes like this:

1) Initial migration of only the ECC database to HANA (ECC on HANA enabled and running on on-premise)

2) Migrate to S/4HANA on-premise afterwards

3) Lastly, migrate your systems to the cloud

This method requires several steps, which can lead to greater overall downtime and may require additional hardware. To mitigate the risk, conservative companies decompose the exercise into smaller parts and perform low-risk incremental migrations: first, they migrate to HANA, then they upgrade to S/4HANA, and finally, they migrate to the cloud.

On the other hand, if you are specializing in cloud migration, you may only need to run the final S/4HANA system copy directly in the cloud environment.

Backup/Restore or Replication

Migration of your S/4 system from on-premise to the cloud is possible in multiple ways. A backup approach and restore to HANA on the cloud is an easy method and highly effective as well. While backups can be large, this is viable with shared storage like NFS or a third-party backup tool.

HANA System Replication (HSR)

HSR is a good way in order to reduce downtime. This is a two-phase migration type where one configures the target environment in parallel and replicates from the source HANA (ECC on HANA or S/4 converted on-premise). By this point, the data is in sync, and the takeover is invoked, the target then becomes the primary, and downtime becomes only the takeover time.

But with one-step conversion, beware! So, there is no way to directly replicate the old DB as it has ECC till cutover. Finally, DB-specific replication like e.g., Oracle Data Guard or MSSQL log shipping will not do as the target must be HANA. HSR is mostly relevant for source systems that are HANA.

Cutover Strategy

Whatever process you choose, you need to have a plan in place to reduce business disruption during migration.

- Do most of the heavy lifting data migration before cutover and avoid a lot of last-minute efforts

- Stop any change on the source system and run the final delta load before cutover

- Always have a rollback plan (if the target isn’t working right, delay changes at the source to be able to roll back to the original system if necessary)

Non-production systems

Do Prepare for migration of DEV and QAS Systems. These are more tolerant of downtime, so you might take another approach, perhaps a simple backup/restore or a fresh install and client copy for development. However, in order for testing to be reliable, your QA environment should contain generic data that is representative of production. This is typically done by replicating it after migrating the production back to QA.

Cloud Database Services vs. VMs: SAP HANA

SAP HANA in the Cloud

AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud: all 3 major cloud providers offer SAP HANA on Virtual Machines (VM) or running on bare metal servers. Currently, none of the cloud providers give SAP HANA as a native Platform as a Service (PaaS) because SAP is marketing HANA as a certified appliance. This means you will need your own HANA VMs to manage, including HANA installation, tuning, and high availability, among other operational tasks.

All the cloud providers have mechanisms to deploy HANA.

- AWS: Launch Wizard for SAP helps you automate the deployment of VMs and the installation of HANA in accordance with best practices

- Azure: SAP-specific ARM templates and Azure Center for SAP Solutions (ACSS) for SAP Deployment automation

- Google Cloud: Provides deployment manager scripts for SAP

But those are just tools to help you with implementation, HANA itself runs on a user-managed virtual machine.

Considerations on Data Migration

If your existing ECC system is on a different database (say, SQL Server) and you have DBAs who are familiar with that database, switching to HANA will force you to build new skills. Ensure your partners or team have the HANA administration expertise. This is an important talent consideration, and, don’t forget, the new platform will require training and support.

Integration with other systems

If you have other SAP systems (BI, CRM, etc.) that are not subject to migration but need to be linked, please note the following:

- In cross-DB search scenarios after migration, performance could be affected if these systems are utilizing old DB.

- Ideally, S/4HANA should connect to other systems via interfaces, not direct DB links.

- If you have been using DBLINK, etc. in Oracle, they will no longer function when migrated to HANA.

- You would have to re-design your database, say use HANA's Smart Data Access (SDA) to connect to the external database or use replication.

Summary

The migration of the database into HANA is the most time-consuming and much-effort-requiring phase of any technical migration, so be sure to plan it accordingly and bear in mind the following points:

- Choose the approach to migrate (Single stage SUM DMO or Multi-stage)

- Try in a lower environment to see the performance

- Check that the Cloud Environment (VM size, disk speed) will allow for the migration to finish within the desired downtime

- Modify database-level dependencies (user accounts, synonyms, links, etc.) to reflect the new environment

However, in the core process, the differences on the cloud provider side are rather minor: the vital aspect is which way the transfer of data is done (e.g. AWS Snowball for slow network speeds, Azure Data Box service for physical transport of disks, etc.), but basically, SAP database migration relies on cross-platform tools provided by SAP rather than cloud-specific migration services.

Research exploration on Data migration in production projects

Multiple organizations have migrated from SAP ECC to the S/4HANA cloud. We can learn from these experiences best practices, common challenges, and risk mitigation strategies. Next steps: lessons learned and recommendations from the field.

Key takes from successful SAP migration experiences

Use a pilot environment first

One of the lessons that we learn over and over again from success stories is to run multiple pilot runs, thus have a new copy of your production environment (or part of your data if huge) to have a dress rehearsal performing your migration up in the cloud. The advantage of this is that it is a low-risk environment which will reveal any dependency issues at the earliest opportunity.

For example, if connection errors occur during the pilot, overlooked interfaces can be identified and the dependency list updated prior to production migration. Cognizant reports on a case study of a customer's pilot migration from ECC 6.0 EHP8 to S/4HANA 1909 on AWS, with process refinements prior to production migration.

Working together between business and technology

Dependency analysis depends on the business as much as tech: business process experts have valuable insight into department-centric processes.

For example, if finance says to us, “We upload files into SAP from external systems every month,” that’s a key dependency to know. For example, we were about to miss a third-party tax calculation engine from being called from within SAP, but a finance user asked that we include it, so we did.

To learn more, you can broadly share your migration plan and actively reach out to the departments, asking them if they have any tools or data flows integrated with SAP.

Planning for High Availability and Recovery in the Cloud

Migration to a cloud environment requires careful planning for high availability and resiliency. In the case of Swiss Re's SAP migration to Azure, the existing private cloud environment had frequent failures because high availability was not considered. They did a redesign of high availability with clustering based on SUSE Linux Enterprise High Availability when migrating to Azure. Your migration can become a stepping stone to a better design - this is the main thing you can learn from this case study.

Remove on-premise single points of failure in the cloud. For example:

- Multiple instances of application server

- Create ASCS/ERS failover cluster

- Implement HANA Replication

When planning your cloud-based application, plan for fault tolerance from the start and ensure external dependencies can failover based on your design. For example, using the same load balancer IP even if your backend changes, external services will always have a stable endpoint.

Take advantage of automation and scripting

Will reduce human errors and be more efficient in repetitive tasks. 95% of the infrastructure built on Azure in Swiss Re's projects was automated, including the following:

- Provisioning of network, VM, and storage

- Automation of some SAP installations

- Use Terraform and Azure Resource Manager templates

Automation lets you rapidly and uniformly redeploy or clone environments for testing, as well as ensuring that each environment is built consistently throughout multi-stage migrations like development, QA, and production.

As a best practice, even spend time scripting VM creation, security rules, agent deployment, etc. OS configuration is done with Ansible or PowerShell DSC, and Terraform is typically used for configured Cloud resources.

Making use of existing frameworks and know-how

Consider utilizing frameworks provided by vendors and partners. For example, the following frameworks are available:

- AWS Migration Acceleration Program (MAP)

- Cognizant's frameworks: cCAT, SmartMove

The key thing is, you won’t be starting from zero: SAP and cloud providers have loads of documentation, and partners have playbooks. Look for architecture diagrams and key points (like network configuration and order of steps) from other architecture references and case studies.

Use early run of SAP Readiness Check

So, make sure to have the SAP Readiness Check run from the earliest start of your project concept phases since it will disclose early "show-stoppers" like:

- Incompatible add-ons

- Large data volumes

- Customer-vendor integration (CVI) need

For example, if you need to migrate 20TB but know you really only have 5TB of active data, you could save both downtime and costs by taking on a data archiving project in advance of the migration.

CVI is about merging customer and vendor masters into business partners and is a mandatory requirement of S/4HANA.
