Doing this can also be a project in and of itself so I want you to at least be aware of that upfront. Many companies conduct these prerequisite projects well in advance of the actual cutover.

Why Integration Testing Is So Helpful

You need to have unit tests, integration tests, user acceptance, and performance tests after migration. You should include integration tests in your plan. For instance, ensure that IDoC submissions from a non-SAP warehouse system to S/4 can be checked that they are processed, or that business users need to verify that reporting from S/4 to other systems comes up as expected.

It is thus the successful migration that is not only judged by whether SAP itself functions, but also how all linked processes behave. In practice, problems such as FTP scripts failing due to DNS changes or firewalls blocking interfaces can occur. These can be discovered by integration testing.

A better method is to prepare a checklist of external interfaces and then test each one methodically as it is available in the cloud environment.

Performance optimization

Following the migration, if you find you are facing performance bottlenecks afterward, it could well be a result of not sizing cloud VMs correctly (too small or wrong resources) or not configuring certain parameters correctly (such as HANA memory allocation and/or Linux kernel settings).

Performing performance testing in a QA environment that replicates your production scale, with SAP standard benchmarks (for instance, dialog response times with ST03N, times for executing batch jobs) and stress-testing critical transactions, is best practice, so that you can fine-tune VM sizes and configuration before going live.

If you have a high latency between the app and the DB in the cloud, you can resolve that by scaling out the application servers or adding more dialog work processes. If you think ahead, resources can scale easily in a cloud environment.

Verifying Data Integrity

Verify that after migration, all data has transferred correctly. Check your Bank balances or pending orders etc. It is important to run reconciliation reports to ensure there is no data loss. This is especially essential if you are moving to the new GL or integrating customers into BP.

It is recommended to run report for comparing trial balance before and after migration, count of specific master data records, etc. It's much easier to make repairs on the fly while the old SAP system is still fresh in your mind (or has read-only access).

Strategies to mitigate dependency analysis

Without knowing that, how much migration can we do? Here are additional better risk mitigation strategies:

Comprehensive stakeholder interviews

It is essential to engage in a dialogue with all stakeholders. Get all teams onboard (Basis, developers, integration team, business owners, infra team, security team, etc. - define this based on your organizations, a short meeting). For this meeting, validate the following:

- What systems are you working within?

- Are there significant jobs?

- Any existing problems (hidden dependencies with manual workarounds)?

Watch out for manual processes that users might not consider "integrations" (a query that downloads a CSV from SAP which you upload to another tool, for example). After migration they also need to be tested.

Allowing buffer time

Add buffers to your project timeline to allow for unknown problems. Even after a full analysis, undiscovered dependencies can be found in testing or in production. For example, it is good to have some time in case it does not have a needed firewall rule.

Use of test tools

Leverage automated testing and code analysis tools:

- Leverage tools like Panaya doing S/4 simulation and detecting errors upfront

- Automate end-to-end testing, including collaboration, using Tricentis and other tools

These tools help identify problems at an early stage and lower the risk of the project.

Consider a Phased Migration

For larger systems, a phased migration may be appropriate.

- Migrate non-production (development, sandboxes) environments to the cloud first - that will allow everyone on your team time to get acclimated

- Closely aligned groups of systems should be lifted/migrated collectively or in a closely coordinated manner

- Identify potential scenarios where you may want to decouple systems (e.g. freezing one while migrating the other)

Develop a rollback plan

Plan ahead to ensure you have a rollback plan if the migration fails.

- Support existing ECC system through Go/No-Go decision

- Prevent or track changes in ECC during cutover

- Run a final backup at the beginning of the cutover so you can restore if needed

Leveraging vendor support

Recognize when you will need to reach out for assistance/expertise/collaboration.

- Engage SAP and discuss the migration plan

- Effective use of SAP expert teams within cloud providers (i.e. AWS, Azure)

- Prepare experts and documentation to troubleshoot any issues encountered in cutover

By using these strategies together, you can substantially minimize dependency issues to ensure a successful cutover.

For complex transitions, use SNPs or similar tools

For migrating complex systems, SNP and CBS are specialized software. These tools can automate not only simple data conversions, but also system splits and consolidations and selective data migrations, and can be rehearsed prior to migration, thus significantly reducing risk. While they may not be significant for simpler migrations, It is important to be aware of the tools available. We've noticed a good example of smooth migration through automation through the use of SNP software, which is introduced in an AWS blog to simulate the migration process and identify problems earlier.

Documentation and Runbooks: Why They Are Important

List all the migration steps, who takes care of them, and how long this will take in a migration runbook. Do make sure you have a step for validating dependency post migration (Example: Step 40: After the migration, check connectivity to XYZ system from S/4 by: Interface team.) It also helps to make sure that everyone on the team is on the same page and that no tasks slip through the cracks in the rush of go-live.

Common challenges and their solutions

Countermeasures to the impact of custom codes

Challenge: Custom code issues may not be discovered during pre-deployment checks, e.g. a little used custom report not being identified in testing which leads to production errors within the S/4 environment.

Solution: Test as many custom functions as you can. Once you have ATC (ABAP Test Cockpit) results, start to address those issues in a systematic way and correct the code. If there are any unused custom programs that might cause confusion upon restoration, then consider deleting those first. Be prepared for ABAP developers to debug and correct problems quickly in case they are discovered after the migration. You ensure the new system has developer keys and permissions available for use by developers beginning during production go-live.

Resolving legacy data inconsistencies and data quality

Challenge: S/4HANA may impose more stringent data requirements than ECC (ex: business partner details may become mandatory for information fields that are optional in ECC). Several problems arise due to poor data quality such as Failed Data Conversions, abnormal processing of Transactions etc.

Solution: Execute pre-migration project data cleansing. This is a great time to clean up your vendor/customer master data and delete old historical data. Pre-emptively running SAP consistency checks (for example, from a financial reconciliation standpoint) is also a good idea. Watch out for errors that are reported during the test conversion since some cases (like resetting open postings older than 10 years in test mode) will not raise an issue until you execute the SUM (Software Update Manager) in test mode.

Overcoming constraints of downtime

Challenge: The conversion time (including bulk data migration) for large systems may be higher than the allowable downtime for business continuity.

Solution: Optimizing each step is the key. Consider the following:

- Near-line storage for BW

- Archive for data volume reduction

- Transition to high-speed networks and transporting data physically

- Parallelization of manual export/import tasks

- Optimization of initial load for HANA database

If possible, think about client cloning prior to cutover and migrating the functions in phases. Also, perform the move on holidays or low-traffic hours to create that extra time room. If you want to avoid downtime to the fullest extent, you can perform a two-stage migration and migrate applications first, capturing the changes in transport middleware; that said, this is an advanced process only performed in very specific scenarios - and certainly not common for ECC to S/4HANA migrations!

Changes to the API that are breaking integrations

Challenge: Integration with third-party systems calling RFCs, which are removed or changed in S/4HANA, may no longer work.

Solution:

1) Use the Simplification List and Readiness Check to specify the required integration coordination points

2) No RFC available to read credit card data, migrate to new API or OData service

3) Pre-configure your external systems (change updating schedules) and run full integration tests

4) Short-term you can activate legacy business functionality using SAP notes or compatibility switches, long-term you will need to change to new S/4 processes.

Lack of skills in the team

Challenges: The existing team may not be knowledgeable in terms of cloud environments and S/4HANA.

Solution:

1) Introduce expert training and support.

2) Include cloud architects in the design stage to prevent knowledge loss to infrastructure teams.

3) Educate your team on post-migration operations (checking virtual machines, managing backups in the cloud, etc.).

4) Changes to the S/4HANA system (usage of Fiori and new transaction codes) will be trained to functional users.

5) Include education time in your project plan.

6) While you're building up your internal team, take advantage of the managed services of a partner in the meantime.

Licensing and Compliance

Challenge: Migration to S/4HANA needs re-licensing.

Solution:

1) To get S/4HANA licenses, negotiate early, and validate cloud migration does not breach existing provisions

2) Discuss your licensing needs early with your SAP account manager

3) Verify that any third-party tools that are integrated with SAP are properly licensed for the new environment (change in host hardware may need new licenses)

Need to work together with multiple stakeholders

Challenge: There are a lot of players involved - infrastructure teams, feature teams, cloud providers, SI partners - poor communication can cause delays.

Solution:

1) Build a strong project management structure

2) Define regular checkpoints, clear ownership (RACI matrix) and escalation paths

3) Create a war room (a central workspace, physical or virtual, for the cutover where all stakeholders join)

4) Real-time collaboration using communication tools like Slack, or Teams

5) This establishes the mechanism of rapid cooperation, such as if the interface has a problem after migration, let the network personnel check security rules and let the functional personnel check customization settings.

Indicators of Success for Case Studies

Swiss Re's Azure migration

- After the migration, availability was improved and the migration was accomplished with zero errors

- The secret sauce to success was extensive HA design and testing

- Migrating a large environment of 170 virtual servers required careful planning.

AWS case study about one-step migration

- This integrated approach reduces downtime by 70% and costs by 60%

- Transformation and cloud migration paired together is a winning strategy

- Balance between risk and reward is important: doing everything at once is complex, but minimizes downtime

- Leveraging tools and expertise are key to success

Do a post-migration review to identify what worked and what didn’t for your team to note for future projects.

Setting Up the Technical Environment and Coding

This section provides example configurations and scripts related to dependency analysis and migration. These are general samples and should be adjusted to your environment.

NetApp SnapMirror for Storage replication (Example)

In the case of NetApp SnapMirror for migrating on-premise SAP data to the cloud, the first step is to create a SnapMirror relationship from a source to a destination. Here is an example of a SnapMirror configuration from an on-premise NetApp volume to a cloud NetApp volume (Amazon FSx for ONTAP or Azure NetApp Files).

It assumes, for example, that you have VPN or Direct Connect/ExpressRoute network connectivity, as well as cluster peering and storage VM (SVM) peering.

On the Destination Cluster (cloud side) the command to be executed is:

# Create the SnapMirror relationship from source to destination
# Replace SourceCluster:svm_source with your source cluster and SVM name
# Replace DestCluster:svm_dest with your dest cluster and SVM (if FSx ONTAP, cluster name could be FSx, svm might be fsx volume's svm)
# Here 'vol1' is the volume name on both sides, assuming you will mirror into a volume named vol1 on dest.
FSx-Dest::> snapmirror create \
    -source-path SourceCluster:svm_source_vol1 \
    -destination-path DestCluster:svm_dest_vol1 \
    -type XDP \
    -throttle unlimited

# Initialize the SnapMirror to start the baseline transfer
FSx-Dest::> snapmirror initialize -destination-path DestCluster:svm_dest_vol1

# (After initial sync, you can schedule or manually update:)
# For a one-time manual update (incremental sync):
FSx-Dest::> snapmirror update -destination-path DestCluster:svm_dest_vol1

# To check status of SnapMirror:
FSx-Dest::> snapmirror show -destination-path DestCluster:svm_dest_vol1 -fields state,status,lag-time,last-transfer-end-time

(The above command is based on NetApp ONTAP CLI usage. In the case of FSx for ONTAP, you get the same ONTAP CLI through the management endpoint, while for Azure NetApp Files the replication is handled via the Azure Portal interface or REST APIs. Consult the appropriate documentation for each environment for the precise syntax, but the basic ideas are the same.)

As noted above, SnapMirror can be deployed to facilitate data protection via the steps below:

1) Configure SnapMirror Relationship: Configure XDP(Extended Data Protection) type SnapMirror relationship between source and destination volume

2) Bandwidth Setting: "-throttle unlimited" option will transfer data with maximum bandwidth. If you do not want to occupy network bandwidth, you can limit it by setting the throttling values.

3) Initial data transfer: "snapmirror initialize" command starts an initial copy of the data. This could take some hours or days, depending on the size of the data and bandwidth of the network.

4) Periodic Updates: on a set interval, run "snapmirror update" command to bring changes in source volume to destination volume (if source still in use)

5) Pre-cutover verification: refresh for the last time before cutover to ensure the source and destination have all relations in sync (no lag)

6) Break the SnapMirror relationship: Use the "snapmirror break" command to break the snapmirror relationship, thereby allowing the destination volume to be writable. Once the break is done, the cloud volume becomes a separate copy of the data that can be mounted to the SAP system.

7) Scope: This approach can be used to migrate filesystems (/sapmnt, /usr/sap/SID, etc.) and database files (while quiescing DB or making use of storage consistent snapshots)

SnapMirror Benefits and Cautions

Benefits: SnapMirror maintains file system permissions, timestamps, and all data identically. Once the relationship is dissolved, the SAP instance in the cloud can mount the volume and provide the same data that it had before in an on-premise scenario.

Note:

- Make sure ONTAP version for both environments are compatible.

- A proper network setup (Intercluster LIF) is necessary.

- SnapMirror on NAT environments (FSx ONTAP has a options for public endpoint, support for VPN as well).

- You can read a detailed guide about migrating to FSx for ONTAP using SnapMirror in AWS documentation.

Example: Network security settings for SAP migration

After SAP Migration proper network configuration is required to make sure that all systems can communicate. Here are a few examples of some security settings on AWS and Azure.

AWS environment security settings examples

In AWS, you can do it on security groups to permit specific traffic.

(Inbound permissions configuration for SAP GUI traffic)

The example below shows how to make the SAP application server accessible from the administration host.

# Allow SAP GUI port 3200 from sg-admin to sg-app
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
    --group-id sg-app-group-id \
    --protocol tcp --port 3200 \
    --source-group sg-admin-group-id

The above command means:

- sg-app: Security group for SAP application servers

- TCP/3200: SAP GUI communication dispatcher port

- sg-admin: Security group for admin/bastion hosts

You can use the security group ID to ensure that connectivity remains intact even when the IP address of your administrative host changes, or you can provide a CIDR IP range to grant access to your corporate LAN subnets, e.g. if your users are connecting via VPN from on-premise.

(Access permission settings from external systems)

Below is an example to enable an on-premise API server (with IP as 10.0.5.5) to connect to an OData service on SAP system via HTTPS.

aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
    --group-id sg-app-group-id \
    --protocol tcp --port 443 \
    --cidr 10.0.5.5/32

It will permit that particular IP - you can mix rules as you like and/or use source security groups as needed.

Azure environment security settings examples

In Azure the equivalent traffic control is done by Network Security Groups (NSGs).

When creating NSG rules using the Azure CLI, the following configuration is required:

- Target: “SAP-App-NSG” applied to SAP subnets

- Purpose: To grant access from the on-premise subnet (10.0.5.0/24) to the following

 - SAP Dispatcher (port 3200-3299)

 - RFC Gateway (port 3300)

az network nsg rule create \
   --resource-group MyResourceGroup \
   --nsg-name SAP-App-NSG \
   --name Allow_SAPGUI_RFC_From_OnPrem \
   --priority 100 \
   --direction Inbound --access Allow --protocol Tcp \
   --source-address-prefixes 10.0.5.0/24 \
   --destination-address-prefixes VirtualNetwork \
   --destination-port-ranges 3200-3300

Here we establish the priority (lower shows higher priority in Azure). This'll open TCP on ports 3200 - 3300 from the source subnet 10.0.5.0/24 (could be your on-premise network via Expressroute) to any destination in your Virtual Network (practically virtually allowing the SAP VMs since the NSG is on the SAP subnet). Here we use VirtualNetwork as destination which means address space of our VNet (virtual network).

Azure also allows for more flexible configuration with Application Security Groups (ASG). With ASG tags, the command can be simplified to:

--destination-asgs SAP-App
--source-asgs AdminHosts

An ASG gives you the ability to apply security rule sets to specific applications or hosts.

Example of security configuration in a Google Cloud environment (Setting firewall rules with gcloud)

Here is an example of opening a firewall rule in Google Cloud to allow access to an SAP system.

gcloud compute firewall-rules create allow-sapgui-from-onprem \
   --network my-sap-vpc \
   --direction INGRESS --action ALLOW --rules tcp:3200-3200 \
   --source-ranges 10.0.5.0/24 \
   --target-tags sap-app-servers

- Basic setup: Tag your VMs with something like “sap-app-servers” and allow access from your on-premise subnet

- Syntax: For Google Cloud, specify port range in the format --rules tcp:port-port

Based on the dependency analysis, the network rules should be defined and should also recognize any interface and user that reaches SAP. The following ports must open on your SAP system:

- SAP dispatcher: 3200 (in case of instance 00)

- SAP RFC Gateway: 3300

- ICM HTTP/HTTPS: 50000 (HTTP), 50001 (HTTPS) (in case of instance 00)

- HANA SQL port: 30015 (tenant DB), 30013 (system DB, for HANA instance 00)

- Administration: SSH (22) or RDP (3389)

- SAPRouter port: Usually 3299 (if in use)

- IDoc/OTC: through a gateway or a designated port

Documenting all necessary ports to facilitate smooth migration and the mapping of those ports in your cloud security configuration during system startup will ensure that your systems can communicate. This will ensure system availability and security.

(Example script) Script to automate validation and check for dependencies

Automating dependency verification is important in SAP system migration efforts. This text presents a practical method for verifying external connections of SAP application servers.

Example 1: Checking active connections (Linux)

On the Linux SAP server, you can run the below command to check all TCP/UDP connections/listening ports.

ss -tupan > connections.txt

This command will filter out local connections and print external connection info with the process name. If you just take a look at the log, you will read, "Gateway process has established a connection to 10.9.8.7 on port 443", for instance. Then, ensure that these IP addresses can be reached in your cloud environment (e.g. via VPN), as well as DNS resolution.

ESTAB 0 0 10.1.2.3:50000 10.9.8.7:443  ...  users:(("gwrd",pid=1234,fd=50))

To obtain a list of connections, execute the following command:

ss -tun | grep ESTAB | awk '{print $5}' | sort | uniq -c

The unique destination addresses of the established connections are listed with the respective amount of connections to each one so that you can easily tell if the server has many connections and you can gain a better understanding of the dependencies.

Example 2: ping test script (Bash)

Run the command below to see if you are able to connect to the hosts found in the dependencies analysis:

HOSTS=("saprouter.mydomain.com" "sftp.partner.com" "10.0.5.10" "mail.server.local")
for h in "${HOSTS[@]}"; do
  echo "Pinging $h..."
  ping -c 4 "$h" &> /dev/null
  if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "Success: $h is reachable"
  else
    echo "Error: Cannot reach $h"
  fi
done

The script iterates over each host in the list and pings to make sure that there is network connectivity.

Note:

- Some servers may not respond to ICMP (ping)

- In cases like these, we encourage you to use netcat (nc) to test TCP connectivity instead

Example 3: Test TCP Port Connectivity
check_port() {
  local host=$1
  local port=$2
  nc -z -w 5 $host $port
  if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "$host:$port is open"
  else
    echo "Cannot connect to $host:$port"
  fi
}

check_port "sftp.partner.com" 22
check_port "saprouter.mydomain.com" 3299
check_port "10.0.5.10" 443

netcat can be used in zero I/O mode to easily check the possibility of establishing a TCP connection.

- -w 5 option: Define the timeout for 5 seconds

- Add this command to a script that you can run to check connectivity to all essential ports.

If you are using Windows-based systems, the power shell provides Test-NetConnection cmdlet which helps you check the status of a connection with the given host and port.

Example 4: Automated ABAP Dependency Checking (ATC) with RFC Scripts

If you want to run ATC checks remotely, you can script them: SAP offers ATC batch execution tasks, which can be executed directly in the system or by using the Custom Code Migration app in Solution Manager to automate them.

(Infrastructure as Code example)

Infrastructure as Code (IaC) snippets (e.g., Terraform, ARM templates) allow you to define the structure of your environment. For instance, you can use Terraform to define AWS security groups in a declarative and reusable manner to maintain consistency in your cloud environment.

resource "aws_security_group" "sap_app_sg" {
  name   = "SAP-App-SG"
  vpc_id = var.vpc_id

  ingress {
    description      = "Allow SAP GUI from Admin hosts"
    from_port        = 3200
    to_port          = 3299
    protocol         = "tcp"
    security_groups  = [aws_security_group.admin_sg.id]
  }
  ingress {
    description      = "Allow HTTPS from corporate network"
    from_port        = 443
    to_port          = 443
    protocol         = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks      = ["10.0.5.0/24"]
  }
  # egress is usually open by default to all (0.0.0.0/0) for outgoing
  tags = {
    Name = "SAP Application Security Group"
  }
}
Other configuration examples

SUSE Cluster Configuration for SAP (Optional): In HA clustering implementations (for ASCS+ERS or HANA, etc.), a virtual IP configuration must be in place to ensure that SUSE Cluster Configuration for SAP stays the same in the event of a failover. With this arrangement, system continuity is maintained following a failure. Swiss Re, for instance, clustered SAP with SUSE HA Extension in Azure to maintain consistency and ensure API endpoints do not change when a failover occurs. In a typical configuration, you define a cluster resource for the IP and then configure the SAP resources as dependent on it. For a virtual IP or DNS connected by external systems, you'll need to configure that on the cluster itself (eg, /etc/ctdb/public_addresses or pacemaker resources). SAP S/4HANA systems are critical to business processes and need to be highly available. It is simply the idea of clustering, where if one of the machines fails, another machine can take its place and automatically continue service, and users are not aware that any interruption occurred. SUSE Linux Enterprise Server for SAP Applications is optimized and certified by SAP for SAP HANA, SAP S/4HANA, and SAP NetWeaver. It helps you to configure and implement multiple HA/DR scenarios with enqueue failover and recovery options.

HANA parameters tuning: After the migration, it is advisable to examine your HANA parameter settings to ensure you are using your VM RAM as efficiently as possible. In particular, tuning global_allocation_limit to be around 70-80% of physical memory and ensuring that auto_log_backup is enabled to prevent the log disk from getting full. It is a critical operational step to keep your database healthy. You can check the status of your HANA memory by using a simple SQL statement:

SELECT HOST, TOTAL_MEMORY_USED_SIZE, INSTANCE_TOTAL_MEMORY_ALLOCATED_SIZE 
FROM M_HOST_RESOURCE_UTILIZATION;

You can do this with this query: It will show you how much memory is given and how much of it is in use.

SAP Profile Adjustment: If the hostname or instance number changes, the instance profile and domain configuration must be updated. For example, if the SID instance number has changed in a cloud environment, make sure all RFC connections are updated to point to the new host/instance. An excellent idea would be to script bulk SM59 connections check.

With these settings and scripts, you can embed them in migration playbooks to automate dependency management and validation, providing a means to centrally document what has to be configured and the code to implement and test those specifications.

Summary

SAP S/4HANA Migration from On-Premise to Cloud

The migration from on-premises SAP ECC 6.0 to SAP S/4HANA in the cloud requires careful planning. The key to success is an infrastructure and application dependency analysis. By identifying all of the network, storage, compute and security dependencies, as well as understanding how all of your applications interact, smooth migration can be ensured.

Dependency Analysis is Critical

- Avoid dropped connections and performance problems

- Dependency mapping with tools like SAP Readiness Check

- Networking, security, storage, and databases are approached on AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud differently

Practical Implementation Examples

- Data Replication using NetApp SnapMirror

- Creating security rules

- Scripting for connectivity testing

Important Points for Cloud Migration

- This is still the same fundamental idea: your SAP system and its components must communicate and work correctly

- Installations in the cloud let you benefit from new methods (security groups, managed storage services, etc.)

- If you plan and analyze it step by step, you can make your SAP systems resilient and productive

S/4HANA migration isn’t just a technical upgrade but an opportunity to re-architect your infrastructure for scalability, agility, and future-proofing. With the complete dependencies analysis and application of the best practices listed here will help ensure a successful Migration Project.

entry.title: CAPturing the Future: AI & CAP
entry.content:
In this post, you will discover an innovative approach to creating CAP applications using AI—without typing a single English word. Imagine transforming a simple photo taken from your mobile device into a fully functional application. Even if the image is just a snapshot of a whiteboard discussion, AI will empower you to convert it into a working application effortlessly. This groundbreaking method redefines the way we interact with technology, combining creativity and automation to streamline app development like never before. Stay tuned to explore the step-by-step process and unleash the potential of AI-driven app creation!

Photo taken from mobile:

I know many of you might be wondering how a simple image can lead to a fully functional application.
Let’s get started…

To begin with, we’ll need Business Application Studio (BAS).
Within BAS, we’ll create a development space using the Full Stack Cloud Application template, as it is specifically designed for CAP (Cloud Application Programming) applications.

 

 

 
 

 

To integrate AI into the BAS application, we need to subscribe to SAP Build Code.
Navigate to the Service Marketplace, search for SAP Build Code, and click on it as shown below.

Click on Create.

Then in the next step, select the appropriate service and plan based on your requirements, and proceed to create it.

You can view it by clicking “View Subscription”, or by selecting “Instances and Subscriptions” from the left-hand panel.

You will be able to see all the services you have subscribed to.

Now, let’s open Business Application Studio (BAS).
On the left-hand side, click on the three dots (menu) and select SAP Fiori.

Note: AI features will only be available if you have completed the previous steps, including subscribing to SAP Build Code.

Upload the image captured on your mobile device by clicking on Choose File.

Select the path and click on OK.

Once the image is uploaded, click on Generate to create the fully functional CAP application.

Here, you can see that the application creation has started using LLM (Large Language Model).
The entire application will be generated in less than a minute.

Click on Preview.

Now, you can see that the application has started.
A fully functional CAP application is available at the assigned port.

Here is the fully functional application generated from a simple image.
• The first page is a list page that supports CRUD operations such as Create, Delete, and Update.
• The second page is an object page displaying detailed information and items extracted from the uploaded image.

By selecting a record, we can also delete it, and all functionality is provided by default by the AI.

Object Page with Delivery details and Products.

Object page with Product details.

Click on create to insert a new record.

Fill the fields to create new record.

click on create.

Now, you can see that the record has been inserted.

 

Conclusion:

So, wrapping it all up, this AI-powered approach isn't just a cool trick; it's a fundamental shift in how we can bring our app ideas to life. Forget endless lines of code and complex setups. Now, a simple photo – whether it's a detailed sketch or a quick brainstorm on a whiteboard – can be the starting point for a fully functional CAP application. This fusion of visual input and intelligent automation democratizes app development, making it more accessible and faster than ever before. It's about unlocking your creativity and letting AI handle the heavy lifting, ultimately streamlining the entire process. The potential here is massive, and we're just scratching the surface of what's possible when we empower our devices to understand and translate our visual concepts into tangible applications.

Please feel free to share your thoughts in the comments section below.

If you enjoyed reading this blog post and would like to receive similar content in the future, please consider following my profile. By following my profile @gaddamarunkumar , you will receive updates on new blog posts and other content related to ABAP development best practices.

Follow the SAP BTP ABAP environment Topic page: SAP BTP

This page is a great resource for staying up-to-date on the latest news and developments in the SAP ABAP environment. You can follow the page to receive updates, post and answer questions, and read other posts on the topic

entry.title: Re: EWM - Auto create Warehouse Task from Inbound & Outbound
entry.content:
@Remy3 , it looks like a LOSC (Layout-Oriented) process; maybe you could just configure that the steps needed to get to the final storage bin must go through an intermediate storage type / storage bin. 


I used a similar approach on a case where, to get a pallet to a high rack, it should first go to a intermediate waiting storage bin - a first task was created directed to a resource like a pallet jack to an intermediate bin, and from there a second task directed to a forklift to move the pallet to its final bin on the rack.

In such case, the system would first create a blocked task from source to final bin, then automatically create another intermediate task (available) from origin to intermediate bin; when this task is confirmed, the original is updated (now, from intermediate to final) and released for execution.

entry.title: Re: create the custom header text at purchase requisition
entry.content:
Hello dear user,

And thank you for asking your question in the SAP Community blog.

in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, you cannot define new header text types for Purchase Requisitions (PRs) like you can in On-Premise or Private Cloud using transaction OMT3 or IMG → Define Text Types.

 

Why You Can't Find "Define Text Type for Header Text"

In the Public Cloud, SAP restricts access to classic IMG nodes for creating custom text types, including:

  • Header text types for Purchase Requisitions
  • Item text types

Only predefined text types are available — and this configuration is not exposed via SSCUI (Self-Service Configuration UI) either.

 

What You Can Do Instead

Option 1: Use Available Standard Header Text Types

You can still use the standard header text field in a PR (e.g., via "Texts" tab in the PR UI), but you're limited to predefined text types such as:

  • Header note
  • Internal note
  • Purchase note

These text types are generally stored in table STXH / STXL, tied to object type EBAN (PR).

 

Option 2: Use Custom Fields to Simulate Custom Header Text

If you need a custom header text field, you can:

  1. Open the "Custom Fields" app
  2. Create a custom field with:
    • Business Context: Procurement: Purchase Requisition
    • Field Type: Text Area
  3. Enable usage for:
    • UI: Purchase Requisition (Header level)
    • OData API: If needed for integration
    • Forms or Reports (if applicable)
  4. Publish the field — it will appear on the PR header.

You can also enable the field for workflow conditions, adaptation, or output forms.

 

Option 3: If Integration Is Needed

If this custom text needs to flow to:

  • Purchase Order
  • Workflow approval
  • Output forms

Then you’ll need to make sure:

  • The custom field is enabled in the relevant objects (e.g., PR → PO transfer via extensibility)
  • The logic is supported via Custom Logic BAdIs, such as:
    • MM_PUR_S4_PR_PROCESS_HEADER

Let me know if you want to auto-fill the text based on conditions, or transfer it from PR to PO — both are possible via extensibility.

 

Summary

Requirement

Solution

Create custom header text type in PR

Not possible in Public Cloud

Add custom text field at PR header

Use "Custom Fields" app

Predefined header text types

Available in PR UI (limited set)

Text transfer to PO or output

Possible via extensibility (BAdIs + enable field usage)

 

I hope this will help you answer your question.

Best regards,

Jeremy

Following the tutorial in the documentation Developing HTML5 Business Solutions as Content Providers, which has steps for Multitenant consumption via subscription. I updated the "sap.cloud.service" in the SAPUI5 manifest.json file, added the cdm.json file and the script on the mta.yaml file to copy it to the resources folder. I also defined the destination templates in the provider side.

After that I followed the Administrator steps in Federation of HTML5 Business Solutions documentation. Subscribed to the multi-tenant app from another subaccount in the same environment and region. Exported and imported the destinations to the consumer subaccount, changing the runtime destination URL to the subscription URL of the consumer.

However after I create the Content Provider in the consumer SAP Build Work Zone channel manager, the content fetch fails with the following message:

"<Connectivity/InternalError>: Could not fetch content due to an internal error. Please try again later. If the issue persists, please open a ticket on the connectivity service."

This is the code I am currently working in https://github.tools.sap/I538205/simple-saas-mt/tree/bwz 
and I also tested the sample from https://github.com/SAP-samples/build-workzone-integration/tree/main/standard/html5-content-provider-sample which also failed to import the content provider.

entry.title: Re: Advanced Bank Account Management (J77) - Create Bank...
entry.content:
Hello dear user,

And thank you for asking your question in the SAP Community blog.

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the Advanced Bank Account Management scope item (J77) has some differences from the on-premise or private cloud versions, especially in terms of UI layout and available fields.

 

Why You Don't See the "Contact Persons" Field

The "Contact Persons" field under the Bank Relationship section in step 4.2.2.2.1.6 (Maintain Internal Bank Account Contact) may not be visible due to one or more of the following reasons:

1. App Version Differences

In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP often hides fields that are not actively used in standard processes or that require custom extensions. This includes fields like Contact Persons.

So if you're using the "Manage Bank Accounts" app, it may not include the "Contact Persons" field by default in the UI.

2. Field Not Enabled in Business Configuration

Some fields require activation through the "Custom Fields and Logic" app or SSCUI / CBC configuration. The "Contact Persons" field may exist in the data model but is not enabled in the current business configuration.

3. Field Removed or Deprecated in Cloud Version

SAP continuously simplifies UI elements in the Cloud Public Edition. It’s possible that the Contact Persons field was deprecated or removed in recent versions. In fact, in the latest SAP Fiori apps for Bank Account Management, contact information is often handled centrally through Business Partner master data, not directly on the bank account.

 

What You Can Do

Option 1: Use "Custom Fields and Logic"

  1. Go to App: Custom Fields
  2. Check if you can add a custom field (type "Contact Person") to the Bank Account business context.
  3. Publish and adapt the UI to make it visible in the Manage Bank Accounts app.

Option 2: Maintain Contact via Business Partner

SAP best practice encourages managing contact details at the Business Partner level (for the bank itself), instead of the bank account level.

 

In SAP Help Portal and SAP Best Practices Explorer for scope item J77, SAP does not explicitly list "Contact Persons" as a required or default field in the Public Cloud version. That step might refer to a Private Cloud or older documentation, or to custom fields added in the past.

 

I hope this will help you answer your question.

Best regards,

Jeremy

entry.title: Re: Cloud Integration, SAP HCI, SAP CPI Interview Questions.
entry.content:
1. What is the difference between Gather and Join?
    Join:
    Brings together messages from different routes or branches within an integration flow.
    It doesn't modify the content of the messages, only their flow.
    Used in conjunction with the Gather step to combine messages from different routes into a single message.
    Gather:
    Merges multiple messages into a single message.
    Used after the Join step to combine the messages that have been brought together from different routes.
    Can be used to reassemble parts of a single message that was split into fragments.
    Example Scenario:
    Imagine you have a message that is split into multiple parts (e.g., due to size limitations) and sent through different routes.
    You would use the Join step to bring these parts together, and then the Gather step to reassemble them into the original message.

entry.title: Split costs into two GLs in one Cost center
entry.content:
We need to split actual costs from a process order (e.g., production costs in CO) into two GL accounts—one for fixed costs and one for variable costs—within a single cost center, when posting to FI. Currently, our process orders come from an external system with a single activity type (e.g., “Machine Work”), and the costs are split into fixed and variable portions using two cost centers after posting.

For example:

Process order cost: $1,000 (e.g., electricity for producing 1,000 liters of coffee).
Desired split: $200 to GL 400000 (Fixed) and $800 to GL 400001 (Variable).
Current setup: Single cost center “Factory A” with one activity type, then split to two cost centers (e.g., “Factory A Fixed” and “Factory A Variable”).
We want to avoid duplicating cost centers and keep it to one cost center (e.g., “Factory A”).

Can the splitting structure (OKES) or another standard feature split the $1,000 into two GLs  within one coat center at posting time, without relying on multiple activity types?

entry.title: Re: Access Control Concern Regarding Journal Entry Apps
entry.content:
Hello dear user,

And thank you for asking your question in the SAP Community blog.

You've clearly laid out a common challenge in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, especially when trying to enforce strict control over journal entry postings using the Verify General Journal Entries workflow while limiting direct posting access.

Let’s try to provide practical solutions:

Objective

Ensure users can only create/post journal entries via the "Verify General Journal Entries" workflow — not directly via:

  • Manage Journal Entries (Create/Post)
  • Post General Journal Entries

Current Issue

You can't remove catalog SAP_FIN_BC_GL_CLOSING_PC because it contains essential apps. But it also unintentionally provides access to:

  • Manage Journal Entries (with "Create")
  • Post General Journal Entries

These two allow bypassing your approval workflow.

Recommendations

1. Restrict App Functions with Custom Business Roles

Even if you keep the catalog, you can still restrict individual apps or actions using custom business roles via the Maintain Business Roles app.

Steps:

Go to Maintain Business Roles.

  1. Find the custom role assigned to the users in question.
  2. Click "Maintain Restrictions".
  3. For Business Catalog SAP_FIN_BC_GL_CLOSING_PC, apply read-only or no access to:
    • App ID F0717 – Manage Journal Entries
    • App ID F0718 – Post General Journal Entries

Tip: Use App Finder or Fiori App Library to get the exact semantic object/action for further fine-tuned control.

You don’t have to remove the entire catalog — just block access to specific tiles or functions within it.

2. Custom Catalog (if needed)

If controlling at the role level isn't granular enough, consider:

  • Creating a custom catalog that includes only the apps and actions you want.
  • Replace SAP_FIN_BC_GL_CLOSING_PC in the user’s business role with this custom catalog.

But this is more complex and should be a last resort.

3. Assign Users Only to “Verify Journal Entries” Process

Make sure users only have access to:

  • App: Verify General Journal Entries (F2548)
  • Optionally: Create General Journal Entries for Verification (F2547) — for preparers

Ensure no access to:

  • Post General Journal Entries (F0718)
  • Manage Journal Entries (F0717) — or at least disable the “Create” function via restrictions

4. Use Workflow Enforcement

If you're using flexible workflow for journal entry verification:

  • Only allow auto-posting after approval in the Verify app
  • This requires posting block until approval
  • You can also enable notification if postings occur outside the process — via Audit Trail or Monitor Workflows

I hope this will help you answer your question.

Best regards,

Jeremy

entry.title: How to Archive/Delete Batches in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?
entry.content:
Could you please provide guidance on how to archive or delete batches in our system?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to Archive/Delete Batches in S/4HANA Cloud Public...
entry.content:
Hi,

Data archiving is a process used to delete data at the end of its lifecycle and to block and delete personal data in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Archiving transfers personal data from the database to archive files once the purpose for the data is fulfilled. During this process, the personal data is automatically marked as blocked. Blocked data is restricted from access to prevent unauthorized processing.

Personal data remains blocked until the retention period expires. Once the retention period is complete, you can proceed with destroying the archived data.

To archive batch master records, you must use the LO_BATCH.

For more detailed information on how to archive batches, please refer to the following resources:

  • 3340679 - Changing the batch management indicator of a material in S/4HANA Cloud | KBA
  • Data Archiving in Batch Management | SAP Help
  • Archiving Batch Master Records Using LO_BATCH | SAP Help

Best regards.

entry.title: SAP Ariba SLP - Optical character recognition and Extraction for Supplier Registration Process
entry.content:
Customer Problem Definition
Currently, the supplier registration process supports the upload documents, such as PDF, Word, etc., which are kept in the different questionnaires as attachments which must then be processed manually to obtain the required information, thus opening the possibility of introducing errors, increasing the supplier registration time and requiring a person to manually review the content to approve it or ask the supplier to correct the information.

Customer Value Proposition
Have an automated workflow that processes the information in attached documents to:

  1. Validate that the information is as required; if not, automatically request the supplier to correct it.
  2. Extract information from documents to enrich it and update internal forms.

In a previous article, I mentioned how to extend this same process but with AI capabilities. Take a look if you missed it  Extending SLP functionalities with SAP BTP and Art... - SAP Community

Solution Architecture Design

On the Ariba side, we will extract and write the information via APIs. This extraction will be done via SAP Build Process Automation, which is a BTP service and already has OCR for our purposes.

Solution Business Process Design

As you can see in the proposed design, the starting point is based on the tasks generated by the supplier registration flow, which gives us the ability to efficiently process new requests and questionnaire updates. 

SAP Ariba Supplier Registration Process Design

On the other hand, once the supplier has completed the required information, the automation can be responsible for validating that the information provided is correct. If it is not, it can request its correction. If all the information is correct, it can auto-approve the process and/or leave it in a final state where a person can make the final approval decision.

SAP Build Process Automation Document Information Extraction Process Design

In the scenario shown, we worked on the premise that the supplier must upload a legal document in this country, which contains standard fields for extraction.

At SAP Build Process Automation there are two options to perform the OCR with different capabilities each one

Results & Conclusions 

The following is an example of the standard legal document uploaded to the supplier registration questionnaire, extracted via API by BTP, processed by applying OCR and writing the desired fields in an internal questionnaire of the supplier registration project in SAP Ariba.

Disclaimer: Test Legal document - No valid information

On the SAP Ariba side, we can validate that the approval nodes are auto-rejected to request information correction and/or auto-approved if the information provided is correct.

More Information About:

APIs

Blog - Consume Ariba APIs using Postman

SAP Ariba Developer Portal
SAP Ariba API Documentation

SAP Build Process Automation

Discovery Center

y_t_afko  TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF t_afko,
y_t_afpo  TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF t_afpo,

y_t_ser05 TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF t_ser05,
y_t_objk  TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF t_objk.


DATA: t_caufv   TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF y_caufv,
      w_caufv   TYPE t_caufv,

      t_mkal    TYPE TABLE OF y_mkal,
      w_mkal    TYPE t_mkal,

      t_mkal2   TYPE TABLE OF y_mkal2,
      w_mkal2   TYPE y_mkal2,

      t_jest    TYPE TABLE OF y_jest,
      w_jest    TYPE t_jest,

      t_tj02t   TYPE TABLE OF y_tj02t,
      w_tj02t   LIKE LINE OF t_tj02t,

      t_tj30    TYPE TABLE OF y_tj30,
      w_tj30    LIKE LINE OF t_tj30,

      t_afpo    TYPE TABLE OF y_afpo,
      w_afpo    TYPE t_afpo,

      t_ser05   TYPE TABLE OF y_ser05,
      w_ser05   LIKE LINE OF t_ser05,

      t_objk    TYPE TABLE OF y_objk,
      w_objk    LIKE LINE OF t_objk,

      t_final   TYPE TABLE OF y_final,
      w_final   TYPE t_final,

      t_makt    TYPE TABLE OF y_makt,
      w_makt    LIKE LINE OF t_makt.

SELECTION-SCREEN BEGIN OF BLOCK 002 WITH FRAME TITLE text-002.
PARAMETERS: p_werks TYPE caufv-werks OBLIGATORY.

SELECT-OPTIONS:
  s_aufnr FOR caufv-aufnr,
  s_matnr FOR caufv-plnbez,
  s_auart FOR caufv-auart,
  s_gstrp FOR caufv-gstrp.

*SELECT-OPTIONS: s_matnr FOR mara-matnr  MATCHCODE OBJECT mat1,
*                s_aufnr FOR caufv-aufnr MATCHCODE OBJECT aufnr,
*                s_auart FOR t003o-auart MATCHCODE OBJECT auart,
*                s_mdv01 FOR mkal-mdv01  MATCHCODE OBJECT mdv01,
*                s_gstrp FOR afko-gstrp  MATCHCODE OBJECT gstrp.
SELECTION-SCREEN END OF BLOCK 002.

SELECTION-SCREEN BEGIN OF BLOCK 003 WITH FRAME TITLE text-003.
PARAMETERS: p_lib AS CHECKBOX DEFAULT 'X',
            p_abas    AS CHECKBOX,
            p_numser  AS CHECKBOX,
            p_prod    AS CHECKBOX,
            p_conc    AS CHECKBOX,
            p_encer  AS CHECKBOX.
SELECTION-SCREEN END OF BLOCK 003.

AT SELECTION-SCREEN.

  IF p_lib IS INITIAL AND
     p_abas IS INITIAL AND
     p_numser IS INITIAL AND
     p_prod IS INITIAL AND
     p_conc IS INITIAL AND
     p_encer IS INITIAL.
    MESSAGE 'Selecionar no mínimo um código de Status' TYPE 'E'.
  ENDIF.

  SELECT aufnr werks gltrp gstrp rsnum gamng plnbez plnme objnr
    FROM caufv
    INTO TABLE t_caufv
    WHERE aufnr  IN s_aufnr
      AND plnbez IN s_matnr
      AND werks  = p_werks
      AND auart  IN s_auart
      AND gstrp  IN s_gstrp.

    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE 'Nenhuma ordem encontrada.' TYPE 'I'.
ENDIF.

  SELECT objnr stat inact
    FROM jest
    INTO TABLE t_jest
    FOR ALL ENTRIES IN t_caufv
    WHERE  objnr = t_caufv-objnr
       AND inact = ''.

  SELECT istat spras txt04
    FROM tj02t
    INTO TABLE t_tj02t
    FOR ALL ENTRIES IN t_jest
    WHERE istat = t_jest-stat
      AND spras = 'PT'.

  SELECT stsma estat
    FROM tj30
    INTO TABLE t_tj30
    FOR ALL ENTRIES IN t_jest
    WHERE stsma = 'ZPP_HEL'
      AND estat = t_jest-stat.

  SELECT aufnr vfmng wemng psmng pamng elikz verid
    FROM afpo
    INTO TABLE t_afpo
    FOR ALL ENTRIES IN t_caufv
    WHERE aufnr = t_caufv-aufnr.

  SELECT matnr werks mdv01
    FROM mkal
    INTO TABLE t_mkal
    FOR ALL ENTRIES IN t_caufv
    WHERE matnr = t_caufv-plnbez
    AND werks = t_caufv-werks.

  SELECT matnr verid
    FROM mkal
    INTO TABLE t_mkal2
    FOR ALL ENTRIES IN t_afpo
    WHERE verid = t_afpo-verid.

  SELECT obknr ppaufnr
    FROM ser05
    INTO TABLE t_ser05
    FOR ALL ENTRIES IN t_caufv
    WHERE ppaufnr = t_caufv-aufnr.

  SELECT obknr equnr
    FROM objk
    INTO TABLE t_objk
    FOR ALL ENTRIES IN t_ser05
    WHERE obknr = t_ser05-obknr.

  SELECT matnr spras maktx
   FROM makt
   INTO TABLE t_makt
   FOR ALL ENTRIES IN t_caufv
   WHERE matnr = t_caufv-plnbez
   AND spras = 'PT'.

  LOOP AT t_caufv INTO w_caufv.

    CLEAR w_final.
    w_final-aufnr   = w_caufv-aufnr.
    w_final-plnbez  = w_caufv-plnbez.
    w_final-werks   = w_caufv-werks.
    w_final-gamng   = w_caufv-gamng.

    READ TABLE t_ser05 INTO w_ser05 WITH KEY ppaufnr = w_caufv-aufnr.
    IF sy-subrc = 0.
      w_final-total_ns = w_final-total_ns.
    ENDIF.

    LOOP AT t_jest INTO w_jest WHERE objnr = w_caufv-objnr.

      READ TABLE t_tj02t INTO w_tj02t WITH KEY istat = w_jest-stat.
      IF sy-subrc = 0.
        CONCATENATE w_final-status_sistema w_tj02t-txt04 INTO w_final-status_sistema SEPARATED BY ' '.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.

    LOOP AT t_tj30 INTO w_tj30 WHERE estat = w_jest-stat.
      CONCATENATE w_final-status_usuario w_tj30-estat INTO w_final-status_usuario SEPARATED BY ' '.
    ENDLOOP.

  read table t_makt into w_makt with key matnr = w_caufv-plnbez spras = 'PT'.
    if sy-subrc = 0.
      w_final-maktx = w_makt-maktx.
  endif.

  read table t_afpo into w_afpo with key aufnr = w_caufv-aufnr.
    if sy-subrc = 0.

      w_final-psmng = w_afpo-pamng - w_afpo-wemng.
      w_final-wemng = w_afpo-wemng.
      w_final-vfmng = w_afpo-vfmng.

    IF w_afpo-vfmng = 0.
      w_final-status_confirm = 'Não confirmado'.
    ELSEIF w_afpo-vfmng < w_caufv-gamng.
      w_final-status_confirm = 'Parcialmente confirmado'.
    ELSEIF w_afpo-vfmng >= w_caufv-gamng.
      w_final-status_confirm = 'CONFIRMADO'.
    ENDIF.


    DATA: l_total_ns TYPE i VALUE 0.

    LOOP AT t_ser05 INTO w_ser05 WHERE ppaufnr = w_caufv-aufnr.
      READ TABLE t_objk INTO w_objk WITH KEY obknr = w_ser05-obknr.
      IF sy-subrc = 0.
        l_total_ns = l_total_ns + 1.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.

    w_final-total_ns = l_total_ns.
   ENDIF.
  ENDLOOP.


  PERFORM saida.

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  SAIDA
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
*  -->  p1        text
*  <--  p2        text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM saida.
*
*  CLEAR:   ls_fieldcat.
*  REFRESH: lt_fieldcat.

  DATA:  lt_fieldcat TYPE          slis_t_fieldcat_alv,
         ls_fieldcat TYPE LINE OF  slis_t_fieldcat_alv,
         lv_layout   TYPE          slis_layout_alv.

  ls_fieldcat-fieldname = 'AUFNR'.
  ls_fieldcat-tabname   = 't_final'.
  ls_fieldcat-seltext_m = 'Ordem'.
  ls_fieldcat-just      = 'C'.
  APPEND ls_fieldcat TO lt_fieldcat.

  ls_fieldcat-seltext_m = 'Material'.
  ls_fieldcat-just      = 'C'.
  APPEND ls_fieldcat TO lt_fieldcat.
  CLEAR  ls_fieldcat.

  ls_fieldcat-tabname   = 't_final'.
  ls_fieldcat-seltext_m = 'Descrição'.
  ls_fieldcat-just      = 'C'.

entry.title: Automatically trigger synchronization of product catalogs in sap commerce cloud
entry.content:
Hello SAP Commerce Experts,
I am currently exploring ways to automatically schedule and trigger the synchronization of Product Catalogs (specifically from Staged to Online) in SAP Commerce Cloud.

I did try this approach

INSERT_UPDATE Trigger ; cronJob(code)[unique=true]            ; cronExpression;relative[default=false];active[default=true];maxAcceptableDelay[default=-1]
                      ; "sync productCatalog:Staged->Online";"0 0 3 * * ?";;;
But ended up in class cast exception while running the job which resulted in the ABORTED STATUS
class de.hybris.platform.cronjob.jalo.CronJob cannot be cast to class de.hybris.platform.catalog.jalo.SyncItemCronJob


My objective is to automate the synchronization process on a daily basis without manual intervention.

 

Is there any OOTB (Out-of-the-Box) feature, configuration, or best practice available in SAP Commerce Cloud to automatically handle catalog synchronization?

 

I would greatly appreciate any documentation references to standard SAP recommendations.

entry.title: Datasphere change a remote table to a view
entry.content:
• I have a space where I replicate tables from an ECC system then use these tables in the SQL data models. 
  • Now I have a need to create a historical version and current version of those tables and replicate to Datapshere using Filters. 
  • Take BSEG for instance.  I will have a h_BSEG remote table replicated with data <= 2024.  Then a c_BSEG replicating data >= 2025.  I will only have to do updates to the c_BSEG saving time in replications. 
  • I need to do this because our version of SAC does not support realtime replication and we don't have anything like SLT.
  • Then I will union the historical data h_BSEG with the current data c_BSEG in a view BSEG.  I will do this all in a different space. 
  • The problem I have is all my queries use the remote table in the original space and I will have to do a lot of work to update the queries replacing the source table reference.
  • I was wondering if I could replace my remote table with a view pointing to my union view.  I know I could do it if the remote table does not exist but I cannot delete it since there are dependencies.

Anyone know a way to replace my Remote table so it stays the same name in the same space so all my queries it is shared to in other spaces still work?

Appreciate any suggestions.

Bart

entry.title: Re: Migration cockpit staging database tables information
entry.content:
The linked blog within the answer is describing the old LTMC transaction and the information about Staging tables is not compatible with the new migration cockpit (MC). The new MC uses different staging tables than the LTMC and the new MC was first delivered with release 2020 and within the Public Cloud. The old transaction LTMC is obsolete since release 2021.

The most valuable information is within this KBA: 2733253 - FAQ for SAP S/4HANA migration cockpit. Migration approach: Transfer / Migrate data using staging tables - SAP for Me

For Public Cloud the correct documentation link is this one: Transferring Data to Staging Tables | SAP Help Portal the one within the answer is only for the on-premises and Private Cloud solutions

entry.title: Re: MDK - How to change the AccessoryType Icon on Grid ta...
entry.content:
MDK does not currently have the ability to customize the accessory image on a Data Table or Grid Table and as you mention there is no separate event for just that indicator.  Data Table and Grid Table have the indicator to help cue a user that clicking on a row will show more detail.

One option might be to put the table into a "delete" mode where a user could select one or more rows to be deleted.  Using an image columns and the row on click to select/deselect the items and then pressing a header or footer button to finalize and actually perform the delete.

entry.title: Re: Access Control Concern Regarding Journal Entry Apps
entry.content:
Hello,

Your use case could be solved with the new authorization concept of App Authorization Variants which was delivered with S/4HANA CE2502.

For more information about this new feature please review the below links:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/53e36b5493804bcdb3f6f14de8b487dd/d45c96e6d9e2426187920bffb3287f45.html?version=2502.500

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/53e36b5493804bcdb3f6f14de8b487dd/e2b39fb01689420393931a9eb3f627ec.html?version=2502.500

Blog Post with details on how to work with App Authorization Variants:

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-sap/precise-control-of-fiori-app-access-with-the-app-authorization-variants/ba-p/13994744

Recording of the mentioned Webinar is available for registered users here:

https://learning.sap.com/live-sessions/sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-2502-early-release-series-cross-technology-topics

 

For the particular use case of Manage Journal Entries F0717A and verification authorization the following AAVs should be assigned:

  • F0717A_TRAN + F8153_TRAN → DISPLAY + EDIT + VERIFY(SUBMIT) possibility
    • F0717A_TRAN - Manage Journal Entries
    • F8153_TRAN - Post General Journal Entries for Verification

Also note, that you should not allow/activate any of the AAVs for the old app F0717_xxxx as it could bring also other authorizations which enable also direct posting of journal entries.

Best Regards

Martin Krucky

entry.title: SAP Developer News April 10th, 2025
entry.content:
DESCRIPTION 

Podcast: https://podcast.opensap.info/sap-developers/2025/04/10/sap-developer-news-april-10th2025/ 

Shaping the Future of Data and AI with SAP HANA Cloud:  

  • Shaping the Future with Data and AI: SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine and Generative AI Toolkit: https://community.sap.com/t5/blogs/blogarticleprintpage/blog-id/technology-blog-sap/article-id/180448  
  • Generative AI Toolkit for SAP HANA Cloud: https://github.com/SAP/generative-ai-toolkit-for-sap-hana-cloud/blob/main/README.md  
  • LangChain integration for SAP HANA Cloud: https://pypi.org/project/langchain-hana/   
  • Invitation to join HANA Tech Con: https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7316037624064819201/   

 

SAP Open Source Webinar Apeiro Reference Architecture  

  • Registration page: https://events.sap.com/apeiro-reference-architecture/en/registration.aspx  
  • LinkedIn post: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/michael-ameling_neonephos-gardener-gardenlinux-activity-7312819875003985923-D7wZ/?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAACgkg4UBF6JXngL8eiZlhbT4UQWinLeQkAo  
  • ApeiroRA: https://apeirora.eu/  
  • NeoNephos Foundation: https://neonephos.org/  

 

CAP March 8.9 Release  

  • Release Notes for March 8.9: https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/releases/mar25  
  • Major Release migration Guide: https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/releases/mar25#prepare-for-major-release  

Free SAP Certification and practice systems for students and lecturers: 

      -  Blog post for student learning hub: https://community.sap.com/t5/beginner-corner-blog-posts/free-sap-certification-and-practice-systems-for-students-amp-lecturers/ba-p/14052493  

     - What is SAP: https://community.sap.com/t5/beginner-corner-blog-posts/how-and-where-do-you-start-a-consulting-career-in-sap/ba-p/221488  

 

Welcome to SAP BusinessObjects BI 2025  

  • Welcome to SAP BusinessObjects BI 2025: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/welcome-to-sap-businessobjects-bi-2025/ba-p/14040695  

 

CHAPTER TITLES   

0:00 Intro  

00:10 Shaping the Future of Data and AI with SAP HANA Cloud  

1:55 SAP Open Source Webinar Apeiro Reference Architecture  

3:13 CAP March 8.9 Release  

4:55 Free SAP Certification and practice systems for students and lecturers 

7:50 Welcome to SAP BusinessObjects BI 2025 

 

TRANSCRIPTION

[Into] This is the SAP Developer News for April 10th, 2025.

[Witalij] We are delighted to inform you that two cool technologies, the Knowledge Graph Engine and the Generative AI Toolkit, have been launched in SAP HANA Cloud. The Knowledge Graph Engine enables you to model and analyze complex relationships within data stored in SAP HANA Cloud. Native RDF triple store support enables semantic data storage and querying using the resource description framework. It also enhances LLM responses by grounding them in business -specific knowledge stored within the knowledge graph, providing more accurate and contextually relevant outputs. A lot of smart words, I know, so you better try it yourself. I plan to make the new SAP CodeJam exercises available shortly. Let me know in the comments if you are interested. The Generative AI Toolkit simplifies the development of intelligent data applications by providing a natural language interface for creating in-database machine learning models, reducing the need for extensive coding from your cert. Although, following recent updates in the long-chain review process, our engineering team has published a new long-chain integration for SAP HANA Cloud package. You can now easily leverage features like in database embeddings, full-text search, and vector indexes directly within LongChain. Interested in discussing this and other aspects of SAP HANA? The very first HANA TechCon will take place at SAP Headquarter in Germany on July 10th. Please check the published agenda and register if you are interested. I'm looking forward to meeting you there.

[Ajay] Hello everyone, I have got some interesting news for the open source enthusiast. There is an upcoming SAP Open Source webinar on the topic Apeiro Reference Architecture, Strengthening Digital Sovereignty for Europe. It is on May 8th at 3 p.m. CESD. The Apeiro Reference Architecture is a part of initiative and aim to build a high-performance cloud edge infrastructure with open source standards. Peter Giese, head of SAP Open Source Program Office, will give you an overview on why and how this project has been transferred into the new NeoNephos Foundation, which will serve as a neutral entity dedicated to fostering, open collaboration, and governance. There is also a LinkedIn post by Michael Ameling explaining the NeoNephos Foundation, where several flagship open source projects such as Gardena, Garden Linux, and more are transferred to NeoNephos through this initiative. Vasu Chandrasekhara, Chief Product Owner of Apeiro, will explain the landscape of Apeiro reference architecture with an overview of the architecture and the components. Please find the registration links to the webinar in the description below.

[Kevin] A March 2025 release of the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model is out. The new version 8.9 brings important changes to you. But before we dive in, I just wanted to remind you that there is a new CDS parser version coming out with the next major version 9.0. So make sure to comply to all the new changes and that your projects keep on running. You can use the new parser now by setting the new parser flag to true to try them out in your project today. So you might as well go ahead and do it. A new change with 8.9 brings you actions to generated child entities when  defining a composition of aspect. This is really nice bringing you action capabilities when reflecting document structures in your domain models. Recursive hierarchies and Fiori Tree Table support is out now available in beta, allowing you to serve read requests for the SAP Fiori Tree Table control. Version 8.9 is introducing a new CDS command, which is cds-up. CDS-up will build and deploy your CAP applications to Cloud Foundry. And if you need to deploy to Kyma, simply add the argument dash dash to Kubernetes to the end of the command. And the last thing I want to mention is that you can now use the cds deploy to HANA command for deploying to Kubernetes as well. So just add the argument of dash dash on Kubernetes this at the end of the command for the magic to begin. And there are more changes in the current release, which we aren't covering here. So make sure to read the official release notes on the CAP documentation page. And as always, the link is in the description below.

[Ebere] Hi, everyone. I've got some exciting news to share, especially if you’re a student just like me, working with SAP, or just curious to know what SAP is all about, the Learning Hub Student Edition is live. And the best part of it, it is free for all actively enrolled students. And here's what you can get. Full access to content available to customers, two certifications attempts, access to live expert sessions, hands-on cloud-based training systems, plus content to help you get certified over time. If you’re wondering, what is SAP? Don't worry, I got it covered. There is a blog post link below. Definitely give that a read if you are new to SAP world. And how do you get started? Let me break it down real quick.Create your SAP universal ID. This is like your learning passport, so  make sure you use your email address of your university to do so. Verify that you are a student using the email address attached or tied with your enrollment. And while waiting for the activation link to get to your inbox, you can already start checking out the student's Zone and exploring courses and even start learning. You can also activate your subscription by checking your inbox to activate the link. If you don’t see it on your inbox, check the spam folder just in case. and you can also start you can only start learning and pick up from where you left or choose new topics from your student's zone account also you can also start practicing on the cloud-based training systems this is really really important because here you actually switch or you turn your theories into real skills and also you can also join live sections and ask expert questions and also learn what is happening in the field. When you think you're done or when you think you've learned enough, you can already book from the Learning Hub certification exams. Remember, you've got two free attempts. Before I go, just a few quick FAQs. Old student email address won't get you access, so make sure you are actively enrolled. And the subscription lasts for 12 months, But, if you're still enrolled, you can renew it. And remember, the certification exam is only for two attempts if only you pass on the first try. So, you're still unsure where to start? I got it covered. You can check out the Discovery SAP for students or position in SAP business suits. And remember, if you ever get stuck, the SAP community is always there for you. For more information, please check out the blog post. Happy learning and goodluck on your SAP journey. Ciao.

[Witalij] Because of all the troubles, I'm misinforming you that SAP Business Objects BI 2025 is now available. But thanks, Josh, for reminding me. SAP Business Objects BI 2025 brings a fresh set of enhancements that make your business analytics more efficient, user-friendly, and future-proof. BI 2025 offers powerful enhancements designed to help you help your business users make faster and more informed decisions. This release isn't just about the new features. It's about ensuring that your BI platform remains secure, flexible, and ready for the future. Please check the Welcome to SAP Business Objects BI 2025 blog post and share your thoughts in the comments there. And by the way, Greg, Eric, yes, I still have it.

entry.title: Duplicate values in inventory ADSO with non-cumulatives
entry.content:
Hi, guys!

I have an ADSO inventory enabled with non-cumulative ratios; let's call this ADSO A. I copied data from ADSO A to another ADSO (let's call this ADSO B), also inventory enabled with non-cumulative ratios.

Both ADSO have a navigational attribute category that comes from infoobjets 0MATERIAL, and I'm using a Full DTP with a filter in this navigational attribute (0MATERIAL-CATEGORY = 9602). Data load was successful, but not so the values; they are doubling.

For example, in the original ADSO, I have plant R34 with a stock value of $345.89, and in the target ADSO, I have plant R34 with a stock value of $691.78.

The relationship of fields in the transformation is one-to-one, and there is not any ABAP routine.

Any clues? Regards. Oscar
